item.dc:title: Carbene‐Catalyzed Enantioselective Synthesis of 1‐Oxyacylated‐2,4‐Disubstituted Cyclopentenes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Base‐Mediated Synthesis of Sulfur‐Substituted Imides using N‐(acyloxy)‐Tetrachlorophthalimides/Anhydrides and Alkyl Isothiouronium Salts
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 19, 22 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
An efficient, scalable, and versatile base‐triggered synthesis of sulfur‐substituted imides with the utility of alkyl isothiouronium salts and masked carboxylic acids under mild reaction conditions.
Imides have a vast presence in pharmaceuticals and natural products, and their synthesis generally relies on the utility of the disagreeable strong oxidants. Herein, we report an oxidant‐free protocol that combines N‐(acyloxy)‐tetrachlorophthalimides, derived from abundant carboxylic acids, and acid anhydrides with isothiouronium salts. Crucial to this protocol is the provision of access to sulfur‐substituted imides, which were previously limited to sulfur‐β‐allyl‐substituted imides and the use of undesirable free thiols. To this end, this developed protocol provides a scalable and functional group‐tolerant reactivity with product yields of up to 96% under mild reaction conditions.
item.content:encoded:
An efficient, scalable, and versatile base-triggered synthesis of sulfur-substituted imides with the utility of alkyl isothiouronium salts and masked carboxylic acids under mild reaction conditions.


Imides have a vast presence in pharmaceuticals and natural products, and their synthesis generally relies on the utility of the disagreeable strong oxidants. Herein, we report an oxidant-free protocol that combines N-(acyloxy)-tetrachlorophthalimides, derived from abundant carboxylic acids, and acid anhydrides with isothiouronium salts. Crucial to this protocol is the provision of access to sulfur-substituted imides, which were previously limited to sulfur-β-allyl-substituted imides and the use of undesirable free thiols. To this end, this developed protocol provides a scalable and functional group-tolerant reactivity with product yields of up to 96% under mild reaction conditions.
item.dc:creator: Malibongwe P. Shandu, Andile R. Ngwenya, Madelien Wooding, Jairus L. Lamola, Paseka T. Moshapo
item.dc:title: Base‐Mediated Synthesis of Sulfur‐Substituted Imides using N‐(acyloxy)‐Tetrachlorophthalimides/Anhydrides and Alkyl Isothiouronium Salts

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Total Synthesis of Novel Oxylipins: Plasmodiophorols A, C, and Epimers
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 19, 22 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
The first total syntheses of plasmodiophorols A and C, recently identified α‐linolenic acid‐derived oxylipins, were achieved from chiral cyclopentafuranone in 12–13 steps. The routes use enzymatic desymmetrization, epimerization, and diastereoselective Grignard addition, providing stereocontrolled access to the natural products and reliable standards for biosynthetic and biological studies.
In 2021, novel oxylipins plasmodiophorol A–C were identified as products of the hydroperoxide bicyclase activity of CYP50918A1 from Plasmodiophora brassicae (Rhizaria, SAR). Here, we report the first total syntheses of plasmodiophorol A and C in 12 and 13 steps, respectively, starting from chiral tetrahydro‐1H‐cyclopentafuranone. Key transformations included enzymatic desymmetrization, epimerization, and diastereoselective Grignard addition. Notably, during the synthesis of plasmodiophorol C, we also generated and structurally characterized a previously unknown compound. These synthetic routes provide reliable access to plasmodiophorols A and C, offering valuable standards for probing their biosynthetic pathways and facilitating their identification in marine biomasses. This work lays the foundation for further chemical and biological studies of these unique oxylipins.
item.content:encoded:
The first total syntheses of plasmodiophorols A and C, recently identified α-linolenic acid-derived oxylipins, were achieved from chiral cyclopentafuranone in 12–13 steps. The routes use enzymatic desymmetrization, epimerization, and diastereoselective Grignard addition, providing stereocontrolled access to the natural products and reliable standards for biosynthetic and biological studies.


In 2021, novel oxylipins plasmodiophorol A–C were identified as products of the hydroperoxide bicyclase activity of CYP50918A1 from Plasmodiophora brassicae (Rhizaria, SAR). Here, we report the first total syntheses of plasmodiophorol A and C in 12 and 13 steps, respectively, starting from chiral tetrahydro-1H-cyclopentafuranone. Key transformations included enzymatic desymmetrization, epimerization, and diastereoselective Grignard addition. Notably, during the synthesis of plasmodiophorol C, we also generated and structurally characterized a previously unknown compound. These synthetic routes provide reliable access to plasmodiophorols A and C, offering valuable standards for probing their biosynthetic pathways and facilitating their identification in marine biomasses. This work lays the foundation for further chemical and biological studies of these unique oxylipins.
item.dc:creator: Alexandre Guy, Aurélien Lebrun, Thierry Durand, Camille Oger
item.dc:title: Total Synthesis of Novel Oxylipins: Plasmodiophorols A, C, and Epimers

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Visible‐Light‐Driven Persulfate Activation by g‐C3N4 for the Synthesis of 3‐Substituted‐2H‐Benzo[b][1,4]oxazin‐2‐Ones
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 19, 22 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
A metal‐free, visible‐light photocatalytic strategy for synthesizing 3‐substituted‐2H‐benzo[b][1,4]oxazin‐2‐ones from aminoarenes and α‐oxocarboxylic acids is presented. Using persulfate and g‐C3N4, a one‐pot two‐step process proceeds through α‐imino acid formation followed by intramolecular dehydrogenative lactonization via a radical pathway. The method operates under mild conditions, is scalable, catalyst‐recoverable, and demonstrates applicability in natural product synthesis.
In both industry and academia, the usage of readily available reagents and heterogeneous catalysts is crucial because they reduce labour and manufacturing wastes. Using persulfate (S2O82−) and graphitic carbon nitride (g‐C3N4) visible‐light photocatalysis, we herein describe a novel and one‐pot, two‐step synthetic methodology for the synthesis of a series of 3‐substituted‐2H‐benzo[b][1,4]oxazin‐2‐ones from aminoarenes and α‐oxocarboxylic acids. A radical pathway mechanism for the reaction has also been proposed based on control experiments. The condensation of aminoarenes with α‐oxocarboxylic acids produced the α‐imino acid, which was then subjected to intramolecular dehydrogenative lactonization in situ to provide the desired 3‐substituted‐2H‐benzo[b][1,4]oxazin‐2‐ones under the condition employed. Notable advantages of this method include the absence of metal‐ions, mild reaction conditions, gram‐scale applicability, suitability for natural product synthesis (e.g., cephalandole A), catalyst recoverability, and environmental friendliness.
item.content:encoded:
A metal-free, visible-light photocatalytic strategy for synthesizing 3-substituted-2H-benzo[b][1,4]oxazin-2-ones from aminoarenes and α-oxocarboxylic acids is presented. Using persulfate and g-C3N4, a one-pot two-step process proceeds through α-imino acid formation followed by intramolecular dehydrogenative lactonization via a radical pathway. The method operates under mild conditions, is scalable, catalyst-recoverable, and demonstrates applicability in natural product synthesis.


In both industry and academia, the usage of readily available reagents and heterogeneous catalysts is crucial because they reduce labour and manufacturing wastes. Using persulfate (S2O8 2 −) and graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) visible-light photocatalysis, we herein describe a novel and one-pot, two-step synthetic methodology for the synthesis of a series of 3-substituted-2H-benzo[b][1,4]oxazin-2-ones from aminoarenes and α-oxocarboxylic acids. A radical pathway mechanism for the reaction has also been proposed based on control experiments. The condensation of aminoarenes with α-oxocarboxylic acids produced the α-imino acid, which was then subjected to intramolecular dehydrogenative lactonization in situ to provide the desired 3-substituted-2H-benzo[b][1,4]oxazin-2-ones under the condition employed. Notable advantages of this method include the absence of metal-ions, mild reaction conditions, gram-scale applicability, suitability for natural product synthesis (e.g., cephalandole A), catalyst recoverability, and environmental friendliness.
item.dc:creator: Palani Natarajan, Pooja, Vipan Singh, Reena Choudhary, Riya Badhwar, Khushmeen Kaur
item.dc:title: Visible‐Light‐Driven Persulfate Activation by g‐C3N4 for the Synthesis of 3‐Substituted‐2H‐Benzo[b][1,4]oxazin‐2‐Ones

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Pyridine‐Linked Diarylmaleimide Dimers Bearing Thiophene Chains: Optical and Proton‐Responsive Properties
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 19, 22 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
Three pyridine‐linked diarylmaleimides bearing thiophene chains were synthesized as a new class of functional dyes. The maleimide dimer framework enhanced the molar absorption coefficients, while longer thiophene chains induced red‐shifted absorption and fluorescence bands. The three compounds also exhibited structure‐specific proton responsiveness and viscosity‐dependent fluorescence.
Diarylmaleimides are promising organic dyes owing to their structural simplicity and the tunability of their electronic structures through aryl substitution. Herein, we designed and synthesized pyridine‐linked diarylmaleimide dimers 1–3 bearing thiophene chains. The pyridine unit imparts proton responsiveness, whereas the length of the thiophene chains controls the electron‐donating ability. To evaluate the effect of the number of maleimide frameworks, h1, a half‐structure of 1, was also synthesized. Comparison of the absorption and fluorescence spectra of 1–3 with those of h1 revealed that the maleimide dimer framework enhances light‐harvesting efficiency and induces larger Stokes shifts. Extending the π‐conjugation through thiophene rings led to redshifted absorption and fluorescence bands, accompanied by increased molar absorption coefficients. As part of their proton‐responsive behavior, acid titration induced larger absorption spectral changes in 1–3 than those observed for h1. Notably, the absorption band of protonated 3 extended into the near‐infrared region. According to 1H NMR analysis and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, 1 exhibited structure‐specific proton responsiveness, involving a conformation in which both the pyridine nitrogen atom and an imide carbonyl oxygen atom participate in proton capture. Additionally, 1–3 displayed viscosity‐dependent fluorescence arising from the restriction of molecular motions.
item.content:encoded:
Three pyridine-linked diarylmaleimides bearing thiophene chains were synthesized as a new class of functional dyes. The maleimide dimer framework enhanced the molar absorption coefficients, while longer thiophene chains induced red-shifted absorption and fluorescence bands. The three compounds also exhibited structure-specific proton responsiveness and viscosity-dependent fluorescence.


Diarylmaleimides are promising organic dyes owing to their structural simplicity and the tunability of their electronic structures through aryl substitution. Herein, we designed and synthesized pyridine-linked diarylmaleimide dimers 1–3 bearing thiophene chains. The pyridine unit imparts proton responsiveness, whereas the length of the thiophene chains controls the electron-donating ability. To evaluate the effect of the number of maleimide frameworks, h1, a half-structure of 1, was also synthesized. Comparison of the absorption and fluorescence spectra of 1–3 with those of h1 revealed that the maleimide dimer framework enhances light-harvesting efficiency and induces larger Stokes shifts. Extending the π-conjugation through thiophene rings led to redshifted absorption and fluorescence bands, accompanied by increased molar absorption coefficients. As part of their proton-responsive behavior, acid titration induced larger absorption spectral changes in 1–3 than those observed for h1. Notably, the absorption band of protonated 3 extended into the near-infrared region. According to 1H NMR analysis and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, 1 exhibited structure-specific proton responsiveness, involving a conformation in which both the pyridine nitrogen atom and an imide carbonyl oxygen atom participate in proton capture. Additionally, 1–3 displayed viscosity-dependent fluorescence arising from the restriction of molecular motions.
item.dc:creator: Takaaki Miyazaki, Honoka Yamaguchi, Akio Mishima, Ryuta Ishikawa, Osamu Hayashida
item.dc:title: Pyridine‐Linked Diarylmaleimide Dimers Bearing Thiophene Chains: Optical and Proton‐Responsive Properties

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Nickel‐Catalyzed Three‐Component Coupling Reactions of Iodobenzenes, an Isocyanide, and a Disilathiane: Access to Cyclic Thioimidates and Secondary Thioamides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 19, 22 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
The one‐pot synthesis of 3‐benzylidenbenzo[c]thiophen‐1‐amines was achieved through the nickel‐catalyzed three‐component coupling reaction of 1‐ethynyl‐2‐iodobenzenes, tert‐butyl isocyanide, and hexamethyldisilathiane. This procedure is applicable to the preparation of secondary thioamides using aryl iodides.
We demonstrate that a Ni(II)/t‐BuOK catalytic system effectively catalyzes the three‐component coupling (TCC) reactions of 2‐ethynyliodobenzenes, t‐butyl isocyanide, and hexamethyldisilathiane to produce various 3‐benzylidene‐N‐(t‐butyl)benzo[c]thiophen‐1‐imine derivatives with a cyclic thioimidate skeleton. The disilathiane was successfully utilized as the sulfur source and can be embedded in a ring structure. Moreover, iodobenzene can undergo the same intermolecular TCC reactions, effectively affording secondary thioamide derivatives.
item.content:encoded:
The one-pot synthesis of 3-benzylidenbenzo[c]thiophen-1-amines was achieved through the nickel-catalyzed three-component coupling reaction of 1-ethynyl-2-iodobenzenes, tert-butyl isocyanide, and hexamethyldisilathiane. This procedure is applicable to the preparation of secondary thioamides using aryl iodides.


We demonstrate that a Ni(II)/t-BuOK catalytic system effectively catalyzes the three-component coupling (TCC) reactions of 2-ethynyliodobenzenes, t-butyl isocyanide, and hexamethyldisilathiane to produce various 3-benzylidene-N-(t-butyl)benzo[c]thiophen-1-imine derivatives with a cyclic thioimidate skeleton. The disilathiane was successfully utilized as the sulfur source and can be embedded in a ring structure. Moreover, iodobenzene can undergo the same intermolecular TCC reactions, effectively affording secondary thioamide derivatives.
item.dc:creator: Norio Sakai, Shun Nishitai, Reo Ikeda, Yohei Ogiwara, Kento Ishida
item.dc:title: Nickel‐Catalyzed Three‐Component Coupling Reactions of Iodobenzenes, an Isocyanide, and a Disilathiane: Access to Cyclic Thioimidates and Secondary Thioamides

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Configurational Reassignment of (+)‐Polycitorol A and (−)‐Polycitorol B by Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopic Analysis, Theoretical Calculation, and Total Synthesis
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 19, 22 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
The configurations of (+)‐polycitorol A and (−)‐polycitorol B, marine tricyclic alkaloids, were reassigned through nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopic analysis, theoretical calculation, and total synthesis. The stereochemistry of (+)‐polycitorol A and (−)‐polycitorol B corresponded to that of (−)‐fasicularin.
The proposed structures of (+)‐polycitorol A and (−)‐polycitorol B, marine tricyclic alkaloids, have been questioned by Kim et al. through total synthesis. In this study, the structure of polycitorol A, a perhydropyrrolo[2,1‐j]quinoline derivative, was scrutinized by nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopic analysis and theoretical calculation. DP4+ analysis of the density functional theory‐calculated NMR chemical shifts showed that the proposed structure most likely required configurational reassignment at the C2 and C5 positions. The reassigned structure of (+)‐polycitorol A was finally established through total synthesis. Inspired by known interconversion between cylindricines A and B through an aziridinium ion, a ring‐expansion reaction of (+)‐polycitorol A under Cossy conditions led to (−)‐polycitorol B. The stereochemistry of (+)‐polycitorol A and (−)‐polycitrol B corresponded to that of (−)‐fasicularin.
item.content:encoded:
The configurations of (+)-polycitorol A and (−)-polycitorol B, marine tricyclic alkaloids, were reassigned through nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopic analysis, theoretical calculation, and total synthesis. The stereochemistry of (+)-polycitorol A and (−)-polycitorol B corresponded to that of (−)-fasicularin.


The proposed structures of (+)-polycitorol A and (−)-polycitorol B, marine tricyclic alkaloids, have been questioned by Kim et al. through total synthesis. In this study, the structure of polycitorol A, a perhydropyrrolo[2,1-j]quinoline derivative, was scrutinized by nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopic analysis and theoretical calculation. DP4+ analysis of the density functional theory-calculated NMR chemical shifts showed that the proposed structure most likely required configurational reassignment at the C2 and C5 positions. The reassigned structure of (+)-polycitorol A was finally established through total synthesis. Inspired by known interconversion between cylindricines A and B through an aziridinium ion, a ring-expansion reaction of (+)-polycitorol A under Cossy conditions led to (−)-polycitorol B. The stereochemistry of (+)-polycitorol A and (−)-polycitrol B corresponded to that of (−)-fasicularin.
item.dc:creator: Kyoko Tatsumoto, Keisuke Murata, Keita Ishikawa, Haruhiko Fuwa
item.dc:title: Configurational Reassignment of (+)‐Polycitorol A and (−)‐Polycitorol B by Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopic Analysis, Theoretical Calculation, and Total Synthesis

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Sustainable Microwave‐Enhanced Synthesis of 2‐Substituted Benzofurans From o‐Alkynylanisoles in Acidic Deep Eutectic Solvents
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 19, 22 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
A microwave‐enhanced sustainable and versatile approach to substituted benzofurans from 2‐alkynylanisoles enabled by an active Brønsted acidic deep eutectic solvent.
The research on alternative media for organic transformations remains a highly demanding topic. In this paper, we describe a sustainable, microwave‐enhanced approach to 2‐substitued benzofurans starting from substituted o‐alkynylanisoles. The method involves the use of an acidic deep eutectic solvent that acts simultaneously as reaction medium and catalyst. The optimized reaction conditions are quite mild (100°C) and the process is complete within 3 h. The reaction setup is simple and does not require an inert atmosphere. Under these conditions, seventeen benzofurans were synthesized in yields of up to 90%. A plausible reaction mechanism has been proposed and supported by headspace GC analyses. To evaluate the sustainability of the process Sheldon's simple environmental factor was also calculated.
item.content:encoded:
A microwave-enhanced sustainable and versatile approach to substituted benzofurans from 2-alkynylanisoles enabled by an active Brønsted acidic deep eutectic solvent.


The research on alternative media for organic transformations remains a highly demanding topic. In this paper, we describe a sustainable, microwave-enhanced approach to 2-substitued benzofurans starting from substituted o-alkynylanisoles. The method involves the use of an acidic deep eutectic solvent that acts simultaneously as reaction medium and catalyst. The optimized reaction conditions are quite mild (100°C) and the process is complete within 3 h. The reaction setup is simple and does not require an inert atmosphere. Under these conditions, seventeen benzofurans were synthesized in yields of up to 90%. A plausible reaction mechanism has been proposed and supported by headspace GC analyses. To evaluate the sustainability of the process Sheldon's simple environmental factor was also calculated.
item.dc:creator: Alessandra Gritti, Angelica Sita, Mehri Goudarzi, Stefano Fedeli, Elison Lepore, Donatella Nava, Matteo Tiecco, Alessandro Caselli, Valentina Pirovano, Giorgio Abbiati
item.dc:title: Sustainable Microwave‐Enhanced Synthesis of 2‐Substituted Benzofurans From o‐Alkynylanisoles in Acidic Deep Eutectic Solvents

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Phase‐Transfer‐Catalyzed Interrupted Barton–Zard Reaction Between Electron‐Deficient Indoles and Benzophenone‐Derived Isocyanides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 19, 22 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
A cascade reaction under phase‐transfer conditions between 3‐nitro indoles and isocyanomethylene dibenzenes affords the construction of pyrrolo[3,4‐b]indole cores. Control experiments provide insight into moderate product yields and clarify why the reaction is not amenable to asymmetric induction.
A cascade reaction enabling the construction of pyrrolo[3,4‐b]indole core from electron‐deficient and benzophenone‐derived isocyanide precursors under phase‐transfer conditions is described. This transformation proceeds in the presence of a quaternary ammonium catalyst and cesium carbonate, affording functionalized polycyclic cores. The reaction follows the interrupted Barton–Zard reaction pathway and tolerates a broad range of 3‐nitroindole and isocyanide substrates. Performed control experiments provide insight into the generally moderate product yields and clarify why the reaction is not amenable to asymmetric induction.
item.content:encoded:
A cascade reaction under phase-transfer conditions between 3-nitro indoles and isocyanomethylene dibenzenes affords the construction of pyrrolo[3,4-b]indole cores. Control experiments provide insight into moderate product yields and clarify why the reaction is not amenable to asymmetric induction.


A cascade reaction enabling the construction of pyrrolo[3,4-b]indole core from electron-deficient and benzophenone-derived isocyanide precursors under phase-transfer conditions is described. This transformation proceeds in the presence of a quaternary ammonium catalyst and cesium carbonate, affording functionalized polycyclic cores. The reaction follows the interrupted Barton–Zard reaction pathway and tolerates a broad range of 3-nitroindole and isocyanide substrates. Performed control experiments provide insight into the generally moderate product yields and clarify why the reaction is not amenable to asymmetric induction.

item.dc:title: Phase‐Transfer‐Catalyzed Interrupted Barton–Zard Reaction Between Electron‐Deficient Indoles and Benzophenone‐Derived Isocyanides

item.title: Front Cover: Metal‐Ligand Cooperative Cobalt (II) Catalysts for Synthesis of Triaryl Pyrazolines and Bis(indolyl)methanes via Alcohol Dehydrogenation (Eur. J. Org. Chem. 19/2026)
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 19, 22 May 2026.
item.dc:description: The Front Cover shows a metal–ligand cooperative cobalt(II) catalyst, illuminating the foundation for diverse molecular syntheses. Radiating routes demonstrate the effectiveness of the catalyst in synthesizing pyrazolines and bis(indolyl)methanes through the dehydrogenation of alcohols, anchored by the crystal structures, which highlight selectivity, catalytic control, and molecular diversity. More information can be found in the Research Article by K. Ghosh and co‐workers (DOI: 10.1002/ejoc.202501172).

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Push–Pull Ynamines and Push–Pull Ynamides: Synthesis, Structure, Reactivity, and Application
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 19, 22 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
Push–pull ynamines and push–pull ynamides, which carry an EWG and an amino or amido group as EDG, are highly interesting classes of EDG‐EWG alkynes that have been intensively studied due to their exceptional reactivity. In this review, the synthesis, structure, reactivity, and application of push–pull ynamines, push–pull ynecarbamates, push–pull ynesulfonamides, push–pull oligoynamines, push–pull oligoynesulfonamides, and push–pull enynamines are comprehensively discussed.
Push–pull ynamines, whose triple bond carries a disubstituted amino group as electron‐donating group (EDG) and an electron‐withdrawing group (EWG), are characterized by a π‐delocalization (push–pull effect) and a polarized triple bond. Push–pull ynamines exhibit the reactivity of both ynamines and EWG alkynes together with reactivities not exhibited by either class of alkyne. Their structure, in particular the push–pull effect, and high reactivity toward electrophiles, nucleophiles, and cycloaddition have been intensively studied. Push–pull oligoynamines, which carry two to four triple bonds and push–pull enynamines, which contain a double and a triple bond, are further push–pull ynamines whose synthesis, structure, and reactivity have attracted considerable interest. A second class of unique EDG‐EWG substituted alkynes are push–pull ynamides, whose triple bond holds an amido group as EDG and an EWG. Push–pull ynamides are more thermally stable but significantly less reactive than push–pull ynamines. The push–pull effect and the polarization of the triple bond are probably less pronounced in push–pull ynamides than in push–pull ynamines. Push–pull ynesulfonamides and push–pull ynecarbamates have been primarily studied in transition metal‐catalyzed reactions and cycloaddition. Keteniminium ions are key intermediates in the reaction of push–pull ynamines and push–pull ynamides with electrophiles. This review summarizes the chemistry of push–pull ynamines, push–pull ynamides, push–pull oligoynamines, push–pull oligoynamides, and push–pull enynamines, focusing on their synthesis, structure, reactivity, and synthetic application.
item.content:encoded:

Push–pull oligoynamines, which carry two to four triple bonds and push–pull enynamines, which contain a double and a triple bond, are further push–pull ynamines whose synthesis, structure, and reactivity have attracted considerable interest. This review summarizes the chemistry of push–pull ynamines, push–pull ynamides, push–pull oligoynamines, push–pull oligoynamides, and push–pull enynamines, focusing on their synthesis, structure, reactivity, and synthetic application.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: 1,2‐Diazetidines − Structure, Synthesis, and Functionalization
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 19, 22 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
1,2‐Diazetidines are saturated four‐membered heterocycles featuring two adjacent nitrogen atoms. Despite recent advances in their synthesis and promising potential in medicinal chemistry, the chemistry of these cyclic hydrazines remains underexplored. This review highlights their three‐dimensional structure and explores various strategies for their preparation and functionalization.
1,2‐Diazetidines are saturated four‐membered heterocycles featuring two adjacent nitrogen atoms. Despite their promising potential as rigid molecular scaffolds for drug discovery, their synthesis and reactivity remain largely unexplored. This review summarizes their three‐dimensional structural features, which have been characterized using various spectroscopic and crystallographic methods. Current synthetic approaches of 1,2‐diazetidines fall into three main categories: (i) [2 + 2] cycloadditions between azo compounds and electron‐rich olefins, (ii) photochemical ring contraction of dihydropyridazines, and (iii) intramolecular ring closure of hydrazine derivatives, the latter enabling stereocontrol at the four‐membered ring. Finally, the reactivity and functionalization of these cyclic hydrazines are discussed, including derivatization at both nitrogen atoms.
item.content:encoded:
1,2-Diazetidines are saturated four-membered heterocycles featuring two adjacent nitrogen atoms. Despite recent advances in their synthesis and promising potential in medicinal chemistry, the chemistry of these cyclic hydrazines remains underexplored. This review highlights their three-dimensional structure and explores various strategies for their preparation and functionalization.


1,2-Diazetidines are saturated four-membered heterocycles featuring two adjacent nitrogen atoms. Despite their promising potential as rigid molecular scaffolds for drug discovery, their synthesis and reactivity remain largely unexplored. This review summarizes their three-dimensional structural features, which have been characterized using various spectroscopic and crystallographic methods. Current synthetic approaches of 1,2-diazetidines fall into three main categories: (i) [2 + 2] cycloadditions between azo compounds and electron-rich olefins, (ii) photochemical ring contraction of dihydropyridazines, and (iii) intramolecular ring closure of hydrazine derivatives, the latter enabling stereocontrol at the four-membered ring. Finally, the reactivity and functionalization of these cyclic hydrazines are discussed, including derivatization at both nitrogen atoms.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Formation of C–F Bonds by Fluorination Using Visible Light
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 19, 22 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
Visible‐light‐driven fluorination reactions have been widely developed in recent years. This review highlights recent advances in visible‐light‐mediated C–F bond formation, providing efficient strategies for the synthesis of a wide range of fluorinated compounds.
Fluorine is a widely used structural element in chemistry and has been utilized in diverse fields, including organic chemistry and pharmaceuticals. Fluorine's diverse applications have led to the development of numerous fluorination reaction methods. Visible‐light‐assisted reactions have attracted significant interest and development in organic chemistry due to their advantages in reaction conditions and efficiency. In particular, visible‐light‐driven fluorination has been recognized as a valuable synthetic strategy, resulting in the production of numerous fluorinated compounds. This review presents a summary of recent developments in visible‐light‐driven fluorination reactions developed since 2021.
item.content:encoded:

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Preparation Methods for Unsymmetrical Disulfides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 19, 22 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
This review primarily focuses on introducing innovative methods for synthesizing unsymmetrical disulfides, utilizing six types of sulfur sources. These methods demonstrate extensive substrate applicability and good functional group tolerance under mild conditions. These innovative approaches exhibit significant advantages in terms of substrate diversity, functional group compatibility, and reaction efficiency under mild conditions, marking substantial progress compared to conventional methods. By strategically optimizing reaction parameters, the effectiveness of these synthetic protocols can be further enhanced to obtain the desired unsymmetrical disulfides.
Disulfides play pivotal roles in diverse scientific domains, ranging from protein folding and cellular redox homeostasis to advanced materials development and pharmaceutical innovation. While various synthetic methodologies have been established, the efficient and selective construction of unsymmetrical disulfides continues to present significant synthetic challenges. This review highlights innovative approaches for synthesizing unsymmetrical disulfides utilizing different sulfur sources, including thiols (or thiophenols), disulfurating reagents, and symmetrical disulfides as sulfur sources, demonstrating broad substrate applicability and functional group tolerance under mild conditions. These innovative methodologies demonstrate significant advantages in substrate diversity, functional group compatibility, and reaction efficiency under mild conditions, marking substantial progress over conventional methods. The synthetic protocols can be further enhanced through strategic optimization of reaction parameters, such as catalyst selection, substrate modification, and condition adjustment, to achieve the desired unsymmetrical disulfides. Beyond summarizing current achievements, this review outlines promising directions for future research, emphasizing the development of more sustainable and selective synthetic pathways. These advancements are anticipated to significantly contribute to the progress of pharmaceutical sciences, materials engineering, and related interdisciplinary fields, ultimately driving innovation in both fundamental research and practical applications.
item.content:encoded:

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Tri‐ and Tetrasubstituted Allyl Amines: Synthetic Strategies and Developments
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 19, 22 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
Polysubstituted allyl amines are among the important scaffolds in many natural products and biorelevant molecules. Moreover, these scaffolds are known for better pharmacological properties, metabolic stability, enhanced selectivity, and membrane permeability compared to simple allyl amines. Their densely substituted olefinic frameworks confer distinctive steric and electronic characteristics, but they also pose significant synthetic challenges, such as steric congestion and difficulties in achieving precise control over regio‐ and E/Z‐selectivity. Hence, strategies for the synthesis of highly substituted counterparts remain limited. This review summarizes recent progress in the synthesis of tri‐ and tetrasubstituted allylamines, with particular emphasis on advances in transition‐metal catalysis, CH activation, hydrofunctionalization, rearrangement processes, and cross‐coupling strategies.
Highly substituted allyl amines, particularly tri‐ and tetrasubstituted variants, represent an important yet relatively underexplored class of nitrogen‐containing compounds with broad relevance in pharmaceuticals, natural product synthesis, and functional materials. Their densely substituted olefinic frameworks impart unique steric and electronic features but also create substantial synthetic challenges, including steric congestion and difficulties in controlling regio‐ and E/Z‐selectivity. While mono‐ and disubstituted allyl amines are readily accessible through classical allylation and hydrofunctionalization methods, efficient and general strategies for highly substituted analogs remain limited. This review highlights recent advances in the synthesis of tri‐ and tetrasubstituted allyl amines, focusing on key developments in transition‐metal catalysis, CH activation, hydrofunctionalization, rearrangement, and cross‐coupling strategies.
item.content:encoded:
Polysubstituted allyl amines are among the important scaffolds in many natural products and biorelevant molecules. Moreover, these scaffolds are known for better pharmacological properties, metabolic stability, enhanced selectivity, and membrane permeability compared to simple allyl amines. Their densely substituted olefinic frameworks confer distinctive steric and electronic characteristics, but they also pose significant synthetic challenges, such as steric congestion and difficulties in achieving precise control over regio- and E/Z-selectivity. Hence, strategies for the synthesis of highly substituted counterparts remain limited. This review summarizes recent progress in the synthesis of tri- and tetrasubstituted allylamines, with particular emphasis on advances in transition-metal catalysis, CH activation, hydrofunctionalization, rearrangement processes, and cross-coupling strategies.


Highly substituted allyl amines, particularly tri- and tetrasubstituted variants, represent an important yet relatively underexplored class of nitrogen-containing compounds with broad relevance in pharmaceuticals, natural product synthesis, and functional materials. Their densely substituted olefinic frameworks impart unique steric and electronic features but also create substantial synthetic challenges, including steric congestion and difficulties in controlling regio- and E/Z-selectivity. While mono- and disubstituted allyl amines are readily accessible through classical allylation and hydrofunctionalization methods, efficient and general strategies for highly substituted analogs remain limited. This review highlights recent advances in the synthesis of tri- and tetrasubstituted allyl amines, focusing on key developments in transition-metal catalysis, CH activation, hydrofunctionalization, rearrangement, and cross-coupling strategies.
item.dc:creator: Aradhana Sahoo, Pragati Biswal, Akhila K. Sahoo
item.dc:title: Tri‐ and Tetrasubstituted Allyl Amines: Synthetic Strategies and Developments

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis of 2‐Aza‐Bicyclo[4.3.0]nonane Derivatives Related to the Camporidines via Intramolecular [3+2]‐Cycloaddition
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
The intramolecular nitrile oxide olefin [3 + 2]‐cycloaddition offers a reliable entry toward 2‐aza‐bicyclo[4.3.0]‐nonanes, a substructure found in several bioactive piperidine alkaloids. The required oxime precursors were prepared in non‐racemic form by Ir‐catalyzed asymmetric allylation and ring‐closing metathesis. Options to transform the cycloaddition products into natural product‐related structures were also explored.
Studies toward the total synthesis of the piperidine alkaloid camporidine A are reported. The core ring system was established through intramolecular nitrile oxide olefin cycloaddition, while the precursors were assembled by Ir‐catalyzed asymmetric allylic amination in the presence of a chiral phosphoramidite ligand and subsequent Ru‐catalyzed ring‐closing metathesis. Using model compounds lacking the hexyl sidechain, we demonstrated that a 2‐aza‐bicyclo‐[4.3.0]nona‐5‐en‐7‐one intermediate, obtained from the isoxazoline cycloadduct by Raney–Nickel reduction, imine hydrolysis, and elimination, can be diastereoselectively epoxidized and further converted to camporidine analogs by Nicolaou–Baran dehydrogenation or E‐selective Wittig olefination. A related sequence was realized with n‐hexyl substituted compounds employing 2‐vinyl‐octylamine (98% ee), prepared by enantioselective allylation of ethyl nitroacetate and subsequent Krapcho decarboxylation and reduction. However, the epoxidation step failed in this case. An alternative route toward compounds displaying the core ring system of the target compounds started with the addition of 2‐(1,3‐dioxolan‐2‐yl)ethyl magnesium bromide to N‐methoxycarbonyl‐3‐(tri‐iso‐propylsilyl)pyridinium chloride to give a 1,2‐dihydropyridine derivative. Surprisingly, upon reaction of the acetal with hydroxylamine, the oxime intermediate directly underwent a formal intramolecular oxime olefin cycloaddition, which according to DFT calculations proceeded as a two‐step process. The resulting isoxazolidine was further transformed via bromo‐desilylation and Suzuki coupling to hexyl‐substituted derivatives.
item.content:encoded:
The intramolecular nitrile oxide olefin [3 + 2]-cycloaddition offers a reliable entry toward 2-aza-bicyclo[4.3.0]-nonanes, a substructure found in several bioactive piperidine alkaloids. The required oxime precursors were prepared in non-racemic form by Ir-catalyzed asymmetric allylation and ring-closing metathesis. Options to transform the cycloaddition products into natural product-related structures were also explored.


Studies toward the total synthesis of the piperidine alkaloid camporidine A are reported. The core ring system was established through intramolecular nitrile oxide olefin cycloaddition, while the precursors were assembled by Ir-catalyzed asymmetric allylic amination in the presence of a chiral phosphoramidite ligand and subsequent Ru-catalyzed ring-closing metathesis. Using model compounds lacking the hexyl sidechain, we demonstrated that a 2-aza-bicyclo-[4.3.0]nona-5-en-7-one intermediate, obtained from the isoxazoline cycloadduct by Raney–Nickel reduction, imine hydrolysis, and elimination, can be diastereoselectively epoxidized and further converted to camporidine analogs by Nicolaou–Baran dehydrogenation or E-selective Wittig olefination. A related sequence was realized with n-hexyl substituted compounds employing 2-vinyl-octylamine (98% ee), prepared by enantioselective allylation of ethyl nitroacetate and subsequent Krapcho decarboxylation and reduction. However, the epoxidation step failed in this case. An alternative route toward compounds displaying the core ring system of the target compounds started with the addition of 2-(1,3-dioxolan-2-yl)ethyl magnesium bromide to N-methoxycarbonyl-3-(tri-iso-propylsilyl)pyridinium chloride to give a 1,2-dihydropyridine derivative. Surprisingly, upon reaction of the acetal with hydroxylamine, the oxime intermediate directly underwent a formal intramolecular oxime olefin cycloaddition, which according to DFT calculations proceeded as a two-step process. The resulting isoxazolidine was further transformed via bromo-desilylation and Suzuki coupling to hexyl-substituted derivatives.
item.dc:creator: Alicia Köcher, Tobias Wilczek, Nora Katharina Korber, Jörg-Martin Neudörfl, Bernd Goldfuss, Hans-Günther Schmalz
item.dc:title: Synthesis of 2‐Aza‐Bicyclo[4.3.0]nonane Derivatives Related to the Camporidines via Intramolecular [3+2]‐Cycloaddition

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Redox Properties of Catalytically Generated Iminium Ions From α,β‐Unsaturated Aldehydes and Chiral Secondary Amines
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
We present a rapid, operationally simple, and broadly applicable protocol for the catalytic formation and in situ electrochemical characterization of chiral a,b‐unsaturated iminium ions by cyclic voltammetry (CV). Using only 5 mol% of the aminocatalyst, we systematically measure the reduction potential of over 50 iminium intermediates, revealing how catalyst structure, substrate identity and solvent influence their redox properties.
Chiral iminium ions are key catalytic intermediates in aminocatalysis, enabling a wide range of enantioselective transformations. Yet, a systematic study of how their electrochemical properties vary as a function of the different reaction components has remained elusive, largely due to their transient nature and inherent instability. Herein we show a general protocol for the catalytic generation and in situ cyclic voltammetric characterisation of over 50 chiral iminium ions. This analysis reveals how the catalyst electronics, aldehyde nature and substitution, and the solvent environment governs their reduction potential. This electrochemical mapping provides predictive insights into iminium ion reactivity via single‐electron transfer, laying the groundwork for the rational design of novel radical‐based asymmetric transformations.
item.content:encoded:
We present a rapid, operationally simple, and broadly applicable protocol for the catalytic formation and in situ electrochemical characterization of chiral a,b-unsaturated iminium ions by cyclic voltammetry (CV). Using only 5 mol% of the aminocatalyst, we systematically measure the reduction potential of over 50 iminium intermediates, revealing how catalyst structure, substrate identity and solvent influence their redox properties.


Chiral iminium ions are key catalytic intermediates in aminocatalysis, enabling a wide range of enantioselective transformations. Yet, a systematic study of how their electrochemical properties vary as a function of the different reaction components has remained elusive, largely due to their transient nature and inherent instability. Herein we show a general protocol for the catalytic generation and in situ cyclic voltammetric characterisation of over 50 chiral iminium ions. This analysis reveals how the catalyst electronics, aldehyde nature and substitution, and the solvent environment governs their reduction potential. This electrochemical mapping provides predictive insights into iminium ion reactivity via single-electron transfer, laying the groundwork for the rational design of novel radical-based asymmetric transformations.
item.dc:creator: Martí Gisbert, Paula Rodríguez, Xavier Companyó
item.dc:title: Redox Properties of Catalytically Generated Iminium Ions From α,β‐Unsaturated Aldehydes and Chiral Secondary Amines

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Isoquinolones in Polycyclic Alkaloids: Structures, Bioactivities, and Synthetic Strategies
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Ring‐fused isoquinolone alkaloids exhibit attractive structural diversity and possess important pharmacological activities, ranging from anticancer to neuroprotection. Herein, we provide a comprehensive review of the synthesis of isoquinolone‐containing alkaloids, highlighting the various cyclization strategies employed, hoping to attract attention from both the synthetic and medicinal chemistry communities.
Isoquinolone is an important heterocycle and key pharmacophore in numerous bioactive molecules, including natural products and small‐molecule drugs. The fusion of the isoquinolone core with other cyclic skeletons generates significant structural diversity, exemplified by oxyberberine‐, nauclea‐, rosettacin‐, and oxynitidine‐type alkaloids, as well as clinically investigated small‐molecule inhibitors, such as indotecan (LMP400) and indimitecan (LMP776). This review systematically discusses the synthesis of ring‐fused isoquinolones, with a particular focus on natural products containing these scaffolds. The organization is based on key transformative reactions, including photo and radical cyclization, base‐mediated cyclization, and transition‐metal‐catalyzed cross‐coupling/C–H activation. By providing a unified discussion of diverse isoquinolone alkaloids and drugs, this review aims to draw attention to advances in synthetic methodologies and the structural diversity inherent to the isoquinolone core, thereby encouraging future investigations into isoquinolone‐based drug discovery.
item.content:encoded:
Ring-fused isoquinolone alkaloids exhibit attractive structural diversity and possess important pharmacological activities, ranging from anticancer to neuroprotection. Herein, we provide a comprehensive review of the synthesis of isoquinolone-containing alkaloids, highlighting the various cyclization strategies employed, hoping to attract attention from both the synthetic and medicinal chemistry communities.


Isoquinolone is an important heterocycle and key pharmacophore in numerous bioactive molecules, including natural products and small-molecule drugs. The fusion of the isoquinolone core with other cyclic skeletons generates significant structural diversity, exemplified by oxyberberine-, nauclea-, rosettacin-, and oxynitidine-type alkaloids, as well as clinically investigated small-molecule inhibitors, such as indotecan (LMP400) and indimitecan (LMP776). This review systematically discusses the synthesis of ring-fused isoquinolones, with a particular focus on natural products containing these scaffolds. The organization is based on key transformative reactions, including photo and radical cyclization, base-mediated cyclization, and transition-metal-catalyzed cross-coupling/C–H activation. By providing a unified discussion of diverse isoquinolone alkaloids and drugs, this review aims to draw attention to advances in synthetic methodologies and the structural diversity inherent to the isoquinolone core, thereby encouraging future investigations into isoquinolone-based drug discovery.
item.dc:creator: Qing Li, Liyuan Su, Xinqi Liu, Chengyang Yu, Xiaomei Wang, Qingfang Li, Di Zhang, Tianyu Wang, Xiangyu Zhang
item.dc:title: Isoquinolones in Polycyclic Alkaloids: Structures, Bioactivities, and Synthetic Strategies

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Electrochemical Cyclodesulfurization Strategy: Synthesis of 2‐Aminoquinazolines and 2‐Aminobenzimidazoles and Investigation of Reaction Mechanism Through Density functional Theory Calculations
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
We unveil a sustainable electrochemical strategy that enables one‐step synthesis of 2‐aminoquinazolines and 2‐aminobenzimidazoles via TBAI‐mediated cyclodesulfurization reaction. The process proceeds through iodine radical intermediates, offering broad scope, high yields, and strong experimental‐computational mechanistic validation.
A streamlined electrochemical protocol has been established that enables direct access to a diverse library of 2‐aminoquinazoline and 2‐aminobenzimidazole derivatives. This transformation proceeds via a tetrabutylammonium iodide (TBAI)‐mediated cyclodesulfurization reaction of various isothiocyanates with 2‐aminobenzylamine or o‐phenylenediamine, forming intermolecular CN bonds in a single step and delivering the heterocycles in yields up to 86%. The reaction proceeds through a radical pathway in which iodine radicals play a pivotal role in promoting cyclization, while molecular iodine itself does not function as a conventional catalyst. This distinction highlights the unique redox behavior of iodide under electrochemical conditions, where anodic oxidation generates reactive iodine species that transiently drive bond formation without persisting as active catalytic species. The robustness of this methodology is underscored by its broad scope, compatibility with sensitive functional groups, and the absence of transition metals or external oxidants, which makes it operationally simple. Mechanistic understanding has been deepened through cyclic voltammetry, radical inhibition assays, HRMS, and electricity on/off control experiments, which confirm the radical nature and the crucial role of electricity in this process. Furthermore, density functional theory calculations provide strong computational support for the proposed intermediates and mechanistic pathways.
item.content:encoded:
We unveil a sustainable electrochemical strategy that enables one-step synthesis of 2-aminoquinazolines and 2-aminobenzimidazoles via TBAI-mediated cyclodesulfurization reaction. The process proceeds through iodine radical intermediates, offering broad scope, high yields, and strong experimental-computational mechanistic validation.


A streamlined electrochemical protocol has been established that enables direct access to a diverse library of 2-aminoquinazoline and 2-aminobenzimidazole derivatives. This transformation proceeds via a tetrabutylammonium iodide (TBAI)-mediated cyclodesulfurization reaction of various isothiocyanates with 2-aminobenzylamine or o-phenylenediamine, forming intermolecular CN bonds in a single step and delivering the heterocycles in yields up to 86%. The reaction proceeds through a radical pathway in which iodine radicals play a pivotal role in promoting cyclization, while molecular iodine itself does not function as a conventional catalyst. This distinction highlights the unique redox behavior of iodide under electrochemical conditions, where anodic oxidation generates reactive iodine species that transiently drive bond formation without persisting as active catalytic species. The robustness of this methodology is underscored by its broad scope, compatibility with sensitive functional groups, and the absence of transition metals or external oxidants, which makes it operationally simple. Mechanistic understanding has been deepened through cyclic voltammetry, radical inhibition assays, HRMS, and electricity on/off control experiments, which confirm the radical nature and the crucial role of electricity in this process. Furthermore, density functional theory calculations provide strong computational support for the proposed intermediates and mechanistic pathways.
item.dc:creator: Kamal Kant, Th. Anjela Devi, Ahanthem Sonia, Singamayum Rabia, Ronaldo Nongmaithem, Gracy Salam, Priyadarshini Naik, Sourav Majumdar, Chandi C. Malakar, Reda A. Haggam
item.dc:title: Electrochemical Cyclodesulfurization Strategy: Synthesis of 2‐Aminoquinazolines and 2‐Aminobenzimidazoles and Investigation of Reaction Mechanism Through Density functional Theory Calculations

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Photoinduced Synthesis of Pyrimidine Thioesters Utilizing Diethyl 2,2′‐Disulfanediylbis(4‐methyl‐6‐phenylpyrimidine‐5‐carboxylate)
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This process is carried out under room temperature conditions and is excited by light emitted from a 10 W blue light‐emitting diode with a wavelength range from 430 to 435 nm, which then drives a photoinduced intermolecular reaction between diethyl 2,2'‐disulfanediylbis(4‐methyl‐6‐phenylpyrimidine‐5‐carboxylate) and various aldehyde compounds. This reaction system employs the organic photocatalyst 4CzIPN and proceeds smoothly in chloroform (TCM), ultimately achieving the efficient synthesis of corresponding pyrimidine thioester products with good isolated yields. Additionally, we have validated its reaction mechanism through density functional theory calculations.
Herein, we describe a metal‐free photosensitization process. This process is carried out under room‐temperature conditions and is excited by light emitted from a 10 W blue light‐emitting diode with a wavelength range from 430 to 435 nm, which then drives a photoinduced intermolecular reaction between diethyl 2,2′‐disulfanediylbis(4‐methyl‐6‐phenylpyrimidine‐5‐carboxylate) and various aldehyde compounds. This reaction system employs the organic photocatalyst 4CzIPN and proceeds smoothly in chloroform (TCM), ultimately achieving the efficient synthesis of corresponding pyrimidine thioester products with good isolated yields. Additionally, we have validated its reaction mechanism through density functional theory calculations.
item.content:encoded:
This process is carried out under room temperature conditions and is excited by light emitted from a 10 W blue light-emitting diode with a wavelength range from 430 to 435 nm, which then drives a photoinduced intermolecular reaction between diethyl 2,2'-disulfanediylbis(4-methyl-6-phenylpyrimidine-5-carboxylate) and various aldehyde compounds. This reaction system employs the organic photocatalyst 4CzIPN and proceeds smoothly in chloroform (TCM), ultimately achieving the efficient synthesis of corresponding pyrimidine thioester products with good isolated yields. Additionally, we have validated its reaction mechanism through density functional theory calculations.


Herein, we describe a metal-free photosensitization process. This process is carried out under room-temperature conditions and is excited by light emitted from a 10 W blue light-emitting diode with a wavelength range from 430 to 435 nm, which then drives a photoinduced intermolecular reaction between diethyl 2,2′-disulfanediylbis(4-methyl-6-phenylpyrimidine-5-carboxylate) and various aldehyde compounds. This reaction system employs the organic photocatalyst 4CzIPN and proceeds smoothly in chloroform (TCM), ultimately achieving the efficient synthesis of corresponding pyrimidine thioester products with good isolated yields. Additionally, we have validated its reaction mechanism through density functional theory calculations.
item.dc:creator: Zhao‐Zhen Zhou, Wen‐Li Zhen, Ya‐Hong Liu, Dong‐Ping Chen, Ming Li, Guo‐Qing Sun, Fa‐Xiang Yang, Xue‐Liang Zhang, Zheng‐Jun Quan, Xi‐Cun Wang
item.dc:title: Photoinduced Synthesis of Pyrimidine Thioesters Utilizing Diethyl 2,2′‐Disulfanediylbis(4‐methyl‐6‐phenylpyrimidine‐5‐carboxylate)

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Toward Sustainable NHC‐Based Photosensitizers: Scalable Synthesis and Postfunctionalization of Fe, Co, and Mn Complexes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A practical, user‐friendly synthesis of an iron–NHC photosensitizer is presented, designed for reliability, scalability, and broad adoption. By identifying simple but critical factors often overlooked, this work delivers a reproducible large‐scale protocol accessible to nonexperts, extends to other metals, and enables straightforward late‐stage functionalization for diverse applications.
Iron‐NHC (N‐heterocyclic carbene) complexes hold strong promise as earth‐abundant alternatives to Ru‐ and Ir‐based photosensitizers, but their synthesis represents additional challenges. Here, we present a systematic study aimed at developing a robust, reproducible, and scalable protocol for the synthesis of [Fe(Brphtmeimb)2]+ that is suitable for researchers of all skill levels. By examining the effects of base, solvent, temperature, concentration, and iron source, we identified several previously overlooked parameters, including FeBr2 solubility, temperature‐dependent precipitation, and solvent quality, that significantly impact reaction outcome. Optimization revealed that low‐temperature addition of LiHMDS and dilute reaction conditions (0.02 M) afford consistently improved yields. Further adjustment of ligand and base stoichiometry enabled reliable scale‐up, delivering the target complex in 64% yield on a 1 mmol scale. This practical procedure provides broad, dependable access to [Fe(Brphtmeimb)2]+, facilitating its use across catalysis, materials chemistry, and related fields. Furthermore, we show postfunctionalization routes of [Fe(Brphtmeimb)2]+ via Suzuki‐type coupling and expand the reaction scope to include additional transition metals such as Mn(IV) and Co(III).
item.content:encoded:
A practical, user-friendly synthesis of an iron–NHC photosensitizer is presented, designed for reliability, scalability, and broad adoption. By identifying simple but critical factors often overlooked, this work delivers a reproducible large-scale protocol accessible to nonexperts, extends to other metals, and enables straightforward late-stage functionalization for diverse applications.


Iron-NHC (N-heterocyclic carbene) complexes hold strong promise as earth-abundant alternatives to Ru- and Ir-based photosensitizers, but their synthesis represents additional challenges. Here, we present a systematic study aimed at developing a robust, reproducible, and scalable protocol for the synthesis of [Fe(Brphtmeimb)2]+ that is suitable for researchers of all skill levels. By examining the effects of base, solvent, temperature, concentration, and iron source, we identified several previously overlooked parameters, including FeBr2 solubility, temperature-dependent precipitation, and solvent quality, that significantly impact reaction outcome. Optimization revealed that low-temperature addition of LiHMDS and dilute reaction conditions (0.02 M) afford consistently improved yields. Further adjustment of ligand and base stoichiometry enabled reliable scale-up, delivering the target complex in 64% yield on a 1 mmol scale. This practical procedure provides broad, dependable access to [Fe(Brphtmeimb)2]+, facilitating its use across catalysis, materials chemistry, and related fields. Furthermore, we show postfunctionalization routes of [Fe(Brphtmeimb)2]+ via Suzuki-type coupling and expand the reaction scope to include additional transition metals such as Mn(IV) and Co(III).
item.dc:creator: Katerina Achilleos, Prabhat Ranjan, Ghena Baroudi, Felix Glaser, Abdullah M. Abudayyeh, Lakshmi S. Narayan, Thomas Thunissen, Koen Robeyns, Ludovic Troian‐Gautier
item.dc:title: Toward Sustainable NHC‐Based Photosensitizers: Scalable Synthesis and Postfunctionalization of Fe, Co, and Mn Complexes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Copper Nanoparticle‐Catalyzed Click Assembly of 1,2,3‐Triazolylphosphonates
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A series of 22 β‐1,2,3‐triazolylphosphonates (21 novel) was efficiently synthesized under green conditions by combining Cu(I)‐catalyzed azide–alkyne cycloaddition click chemistry and organophosphorus methodologies. The compounds were fully characterized and evaluated as cholinesterase inhibitors, showing notable selectivity toward butyrylcholinesterase. Among them, 4ack stood out for its strong activity and significantly lower neurotoxicity, highlighting the potential of this scaffold for further exploration.
The development of efficient and sustainable synthetic methodologies for accessing structurally complex molecules remains a key objective in organic chemistry. Herein, we report the design and synthesis of a library of 22 β‐1,2,3‐triazolylphosphonate derivatives, 21 previously unreported, obtained through the integration of robust synthetic strategies under green conditions. The 1,2,3‐triazole scaffold was assembled via a Cu(I)‐catalyzed azide–alkyne cycloaddition click reaction using supported copper nanoparticles as an efficient and recyclable catalyst, while the phosphonate fragments were introduced through established organophosphorus methodologies. All compounds were fully characterized and evaluated for their in vitro acetyl‐ and butyrylcholinesterase (AChE and BChE) inhibitory activity. The majority of the derivatives exhibited pronounced selectivity toward BChE inhibition, with compounds 4aca and 4ack emerging as the most potent inhibitors (IC50 = 2.7 and 2.9 µM, respectively). Notably, compound 4ack showed significantly lower neurotoxicity in N27 cells compared to 4aca, displaying a two‐order‐of‐magnitude separation between its BChE inhibitory potency and cytotoxic effects (IC50 = 255.3 and 37.3 µM, respectively). In silico absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion predictions were performed for all derivatives. Collectively, these results highlight β‐1,2,3‐triazolylphosphonates as a versatile chemical platform and underscore the effectiveness of combining click chemistry with organophosphorus approaches for the generation of structurally diverse bioactive candidates.
item.content:encoded:
A series of 22 β-1,2,3-triazolylphosphonates (21 novel) was efficiently synthesized under green conditions by combining Cu(I)-catalyzed azide–alkyne cycloaddition click chemistry and organophosphorus methodologies. The compounds were fully characterized and evaluated as cholinesterase inhibitors, showing notable selectivity toward butyrylcholinesterase. Among them, 4ack stood out for its strong activity and significantly lower neurotoxicity, highlighting the potential of this scaffold for further exploration.


The development of efficient and sustainable synthetic methodologies for accessing structurally complex molecules remains a key objective in organic chemistry. Herein, we report the design and synthesis of a library of 22 β-1,2,3-triazolylphosphonate derivatives, 21 previously unreported, obtained through the integration of robust synthetic strategies under green conditions. The 1,2,3-triazole scaffold was assembled via a Cu(I)-catalyzed azide–alkyne cycloaddition click reaction using supported copper nanoparticles as an efficient and recyclable catalyst, while the phosphonate fragments were introduced through established organophosphorus methodologies. All compounds were fully characterized and evaluated for their in vitro acetyl- and butyrylcholinesterase (AChE and BChE) inhibitory activity. The majority of the derivatives exhibited pronounced selectivity toward BChE inhibition, with compounds 4aca and 4ack emerging as the most potent inhibitors (IC50 = 2.7 and 2.9 µM, respectively). Notably, compound 4ack showed significantly lower neurotoxicity in N27 cells compared to 4aca, displaying a two-order-of-magnitude separation between its BChE inhibitory potency and cytotoxic effects (IC50 = 255.3 and 37.3 µM, respectively). In silico absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion predictions were performed for all derivatives. Collectively, these results highlight β-1,2,3-triazolylphosphonates as a versatile chemical platform and underscore the effectiveness of combining click chemistry with organophosphorus approaches for the generation of structurally diverse bioactive candidates.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Ligand‐Enabled Pd‐Catalyzed Enantioselective C−H Amination for the Synthesis of Axially Chiral Biaryl Diamines
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A new class of axially chiral biaryl diamine ligands possessing a binaphthyl skeleton was developed to promote the challenging Pd‐catalyzed enantioselective C−H amination of biaryl amine under very mild conditions. This protocol provides direct access to axially chiral biaryl diamine derivatives in up to 79% yield with up to 90.5:9.5 enantiomeric ratio (er).
Pd‐catalyzed enantioselective C−H amination of biaryl amines with morpholino benzoate has been achieved using a novel class of axially chiral diamine ligands featuring a binaphthyl skeleton. The reaction proceeds under very mild conditions and exhibits good functional group tolerance, delivering a range of axially chiral biaryl diamine derivatives bearing a morpholine unit in moderate to good yields (up to 79%) and with good enantioselectivity (up to 90.5:9.5 enantiomeric ratio (er)).
item.content:encoded:
A new class of axially chiral biaryl diamine ligands possessing a binaphthyl skeleton was developed to promote the challenging Pd-catalyzed enantioselective C−H amination of biaryl amine under very mild conditions. This protocol provides direct access to axially chiral biaryl diamine derivatives in up to 79% yield with up to 90.5:9.5 enantiomeric ratio (er).


Pd-catalyzed enantioselective C−H amination of biaryl amines with morpholino benzoate has been achieved using a novel class of axially chiral diamine ligands featuring a binaphthyl skeleton. The reaction proceeds under very mild conditions and exhibits good functional group tolerance, delivering a range of axially chiral biaryl diamine derivatives bearing a morpholine unit in moderate to good yields (up to 79%) and with good enantioselectivity (up to 90.5:9.5 enantiomeric ratio (er)).

item.dc:title: Ligand‐Enabled Pd‐Catalyzed Enantioselective C−H Amination for the Synthesis of Axially Chiral Biaryl Diamines

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Darzens Reaction of 2,2,2‐Trifluoroacetophenones With α‐Fluoroindanones
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Darzens condensations of 2,2,2‐trifluoroacetophenones with α‐fluoroindanones were developed. A variety of tetrasubstituted α, β‐epoxyketones were constructed using this protocol in moderate to good yields (up to 76%), with moderate to excellent diastereoselectivities (up to 94:6 dr).
Darzens condensations of 2,2,2‐trifluoroacetophenones with α‐fluoroindanones were developed. A variety of tetrasubstituted α,β‐epoxyketones were constructed using this protocol in moderate to good yields (up to 76%), with moderate to excellent diastereoselectivities (up to 94:6 dr).
item.content:encoded:
Darzens condensations of 2,2,2-trifluoroacetophenones with α-fluoroindanones were developed. A variety of tetrasubstituted α, β-epoxyketones were constructed using this protocol in moderate to good yields (up to 76%), with moderate to excellent diastereoselectivities (up to 94:6 dr).
