item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Chemoenzymatic Synthesis of 5,11‐Epoxy‐4‐Hydroxyamoyphane, 4,11‐Epoxy‐5‐Hydroxyamoyphane and Their Structural Reassignment
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Chemoenzymatic synthesis of two sesquiterpene ethers was achieved featuring a heterologous synthesis of amorphane skeleton and a biomimetic epoxide ring‐opening process. The originally proposed tetrahydropyran containing structure was revised to 5,11‐epoxy‐4‐hydroxyamoyphane containing a tetrahydrofuran ring on the basis of X‐ray diffraction analysis of its para‐nitrobenzoyl ester derivative.
A chemoenzymatic synthesis of both 4,11‐epoxy‐5‐hydroxyamoyphane (1) and 5,11‐epoxy‐4‐hydroxyamoyphane (2) was achieved. The amorpha‐4,11‐diene containing the amorphane skeleton was obtained in 100 mg/L by supplementing prenol/isoprenol to an engineered E. coli harboring the isopentenol utilization pathway and amorpha‐4,11‐diene synthase (ADS). Selective epoxidation and hydration of the two double bonds of amorpha‐4,11‐diene, followed by a biomimetic epoxide ring‐opening process, allowed the synthesis of both 1 and 2. The structure of 2, containing the tetrahydrofuran ring, was further confirmed by X‐ray crystal diffraction analysis of its para‐nitrobenzoyl derivative. The structure of the natural product isolated from Fabiana imbricata, originally elucidated as 1 containing a tetrahydropyran ring, was thus revised to 2.
item.content:encoded:
Chemoenzymatic synthesis of two sesquiterpene ethers was achieved featuring a heterologous synthesis of amorphane skeleton and a biomimetic epoxide ring-opening process. The originally proposed tetrahydropyran containing structure was revised to 5,11-epoxy-4-hydroxyamoyphane containing a tetrahydrofuran ring on the basis of X-ray diffraction analysis of its para-nitrobenzoyl ester derivative.


A chemoenzymatic synthesis of both 4,11-epoxy-5-hydroxyamoyphane (1) and 5,11-epoxy-4-hydroxyamoyphane (2) was achieved. The amorpha-4,11-diene containing the amorphane skeleton was obtained in 100 mg/L by supplementing prenol/isoprenol to an engineered E. coli harboring the isopentenol utilization pathway and amorpha-4,11-diene synthase (ADS). Selective epoxidation and hydration of the two double bonds of amorpha-4,11-diene, followed by a biomimetic epoxide ring-opening process, allowed the synthesis of both 1 and 2. The structure of 2, containing the tetrahydrofuran ring, was further confirmed by X-ray crystal diffraction analysis of its para-nitrobenzoyl derivative. The structure of the natural product isolated from Fabiana imbricata, originally elucidated as 1 containing a tetrahydropyran ring, was thus revised to 2.
item.dc:creator: Tao Wang, Kaibiao Wang, Zhengren Xu
item.dc:title: Chemoenzymatic Synthesis of 5,11‐Epoxy‐4‐Hydroxyamoyphane, 4,11‐Epoxy‐5‐Hydroxyamoyphane and Their Structural Reassignment

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Lithium Diisopropylamide‐Mediated Ring‐Opening of Tetrahydrofuran in the Synthesis of Polyhydroxylated Indolizidines
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Chiral tri‐ and pentahydroxy spirocyclopentane‐indolizidines were synthesized using lithium diisopropylamide “LDA” promoted diallylation and tetrahydrofuran “THF” ring‐opening in a one‐pot procedure as a key step. The sequence leading to the targeted hydroxy‐spiroindolizidines was achieved by cis‐dihydroxylation followed by reduction of the enamine and lactam in the precursors.
A synthesis of novel hydroxyethyl‐substituted tri‐ and penta‐hydroxylated spiro[cyclopentane‐indolizidines] has been reported, featuring an interesting lithium diisopropylamide “LDA”‐mediated α‐diallylation and tetrahydrofuran “THF” ring opening in one‐pot procedure as the key transformation. The synthetic sequence explored herein uses an enantiopure tricyclic lactam as a common chiral precursor, prepared on a gram scale from cost‐effective basic reagents. The key steps in this synthetic way included diastereoselective dihydroxylation reaction, ring‐closing ene–ene metathesis, enamine reduction, and finally lactam reduction. These transformations enabled the efficient and stereoselective construction of the ultimate polyhydroxy‐indolizidine scaffolds. As an application, the biological activity of the synthesized spiro‐indolizidines was evaluated against a panel of α‐glucosidases.
item.content:encoded:
Chiral tri- and pentahydroxy spirocyclopentane-indolizidines were synthesized using lithium diisopropylamide “LDA” promoted diallylation and tetrahydrofuran “THF” ring-opening in a one-pot procedure as a key step. The sequence leading to the targeted hydroxy-spiroindolizidines was achieved by cis-dihydroxylation followed by reduction of the enamine and lactam in the precursors.


A synthesis of novel hydroxyethyl-substituted tri- and penta-hydroxylated spiro[cyclopentane-indolizidines] has been reported, featuring an interesting lithium diisopropylamide “LDA”-mediated α-diallylation and tetrahydrofuran “THF” ring opening in one-pot procedure as the key transformation. The synthetic sequence explored herein uses an enantiopure tricyclic lactam as a common chiral precursor, prepared on a gram scale from cost-effective basic reagents. The key steps in this synthetic way included diastereoselective dihydroxylation reaction, ring-closing ene–ene metathesis, enamine reduction, and finally lactam reduction. These transformations enabled the efficient and stereoselective construction of the ultimate polyhydroxy-indolizidine scaffolds. As an application, the biological activity of the synthesized spiro-indolizidines was evaluated against a panel of α-glucosidases.
item.dc:creator: Paula Fraňová, Peter Šafář, Marchalín Štefan, Petra Pažitná, Ivana Žídeková, Ján Moncol, Adam Daïch
item.dc:title: Lithium Diisopropylamide‐Mediated Ring‐Opening of Tetrahydrofuran in the Synthesis of Polyhydroxylated Indolizidines

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Copper‐Catalyzed N‐Alkenylation of Azidoformates With Alkenyl Boronic Acids: Synthesis of N‐Alkenylcarbamates
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A CuCl‐catalyzed N‐alkenylation of azidoformates with diverse alkenyl boronic acids is described. The transformation proceeds under mild, ligand‐free conditions in air at room temperature and accommodates styryl, α‐substituted, π‐extended, and cyclic substrates. Control experiments support a nitrene‐type pathway, providing efficient access to N‐alkenylcarbamates.
We report a copper‐catalyzed N‐alkenylation of azidoformates with a range of alkenyl boronic acids, providing an efficient and practical method for the synthesis of N‐alkenylcarbamates. The reaction proceeds at room temperature in an open flask using CuCl as the sole catalyst, without the need for added base or ligand. A broad spectrum of alkenyl boronic acids, including styryl, α‐substituted, cyclic, and heteroaryl derivatives, couples smoothly with diverse azidoformates to afford the corresponding enecarbamates in good to excellent yields. The method is operationally simple and readily scalable. Representative downstream transformations further demonstrate the synthetic utility of the products. Control experiments are consistent with the involvement of a nitrene‐type intermediate in the reaction pathway.
item.content:encoded:
A CuCl-catalyzed N-alkenylation of azidoformates with diverse alkenyl boronic acids is described. The transformation proceeds under mild, ligand-free conditions in air at room temperature and accommodates styryl, α-substituted, π-extended, and cyclic substrates. Control experiments support a nitrene-type pathway, providing efficient access to N-alkenylcarbamates.


We report a copper-catalyzed N-alkenylation of azidoformates with a range of alkenyl boronic acids, providing an efficient and practical method for the synthesis of N-alkenylcarbamates. The reaction proceeds at room temperature in an open flask using CuCl as the sole catalyst, without the need for added base or ligand. A broad spectrum of alkenyl boronic acids, including styryl, α-substituted, cyclic, and heteroaryl derivatives, couples smoothly with diverse azidoformates to afford the corresponding enecarbamates in good to excellent yields. The method is operationally simple and readily scalable. Representative downstream transformations further demonstrate the synthetic utility of the products. Control experiments are consistent with the involvement of a nitrene-type intermediate in the reaction pathway.
item.dc:creator: Jaehoon Lee, Ha‐Young Ryoo, Min‐Jung Lee, Inji Shin, Won‐Suk Kim
item.dc:title: Copper‐Catalyzed N‐Alkenylation of Azidoformates With Alkenyl Boronic Acids: Synthesis of N‐Alkenylcarbamates

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis and Reactivity of Iodepinium Salts
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
An efficient oxidative cyclization of (Z)‐iodostilbenes grants access to the long‐elusive class of seven‐membered diaryliodonium salts — the iodepinium triflates. X‐ray analysis reveals their distinctly nonplanar geometry, while reactivity studies uncover unprecedented pathways, including copper‐mediated conversion to phenanthrene and chalcogen‐induced ring contraction to 1‐iodophenanthrene.
Seven‐membered cyclic diaryliodonium salts, known as iodepinium salts, remain a significantly underexplored class of hypervalent iodine compounds. This scarcity is primarily attributed to the lack of efficient synthetic methodologies. Herein, we report an effective protocol for the synthesis of iodepinium triflates via the oxidative cyclization of iodinated (Z)‐stilbenes. In addition to establishing a substrate scope of 10 derivatives with yields up to 83%, the structural features of the unique seven‐membered ring were elucidated via X‐ray crystallography. Furthermore, initial investigations into their reactivity reveal unique decomposition and transformation pathways.
item.content:encoded:
An efficient oxidative cyclization of (Z)-iodostilbenes grants access to the long-elusive class of seven-membered diaryliodonium salts — the iodepinium triflates. X-ray analysis reveals their distinctly nonplanar geometry, while reactivity studies uncover unprecedented pathways, including copper-mediated conversion to phenanthrene and chalcogen-induced ring contraction to 1-iodophenanthrene.


Seven-membered cyclic diaryliodonium salts, known as iodepinium salts, remain a significantly underexplored class of hypervalent iodine compounds. This scarcity is primarily attributed to the lack of efficient synthetic methodologies. Herein, we report an effective protocol for the synthesis of iodepinium triflates via the oxidative cyclization of iodinated (Z)-stilbenes. In addition to establishing a substrate scope of 10 derivatives with yields up to 83%, the structural features of the unique seven-membered ring were elucidated via X-ray crystallography. Furthermore, initial investigations into their reactivity reveal unique decomposition and transformation pathways.
item.dc:creator: Jan Rick Koch, Mattis Damrath, Pim Puylaert, Boris J. Nachtsheim
item.dc:title: Synthesis and Reactivity of Iodepinium Salts

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Isotellurourea‐Catalyzed Enantioselective (4+2)‐Heterocycloadditions of But‐2‐ynoates and Michael Acceptors
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Isotelluroureas allow for the use of alkynoates for asymmetric (4+2)‐heterocycloadditions with different Michael acceptors.
Alk‐2‐ynoates are readily available starting materials which, in principle, can be utilized for cycloadditions in analogy to structurally similar allenoates. However, their significantly lower reactivity makes activation by established isothiourea and isoselenourea Lewis base catalysts difficult. Capitalizing on the higher nucleophilicity and basicity of our recently introduced class of isotellurourea catalysts we now succeeded in successfully employing alk‐2‐ynoates for asymmetric (4 + 2)‐heterocycloadditions as well, resulting in even improved enantioselectivities as compared to the use of analogous allenoates. These results demonstrate the high potential of the highly reactive isotelluroureas for the catalytic activation of less reactive starting materials where established catalyst classes come to their limits.
item.content:encoded:
Isotelluroureas allow for the use of alkynoates for asymmetric (4+2)-heterocycloadditions with different Michael acceptors.


Alk-2-ynoates are readily available starting materials which, in principle, can be utilized for cycloadditions in analogy to structurally similar allenoates. However, their significantly lower reactivity makes activation by established isothiourea and isoselenourea Lewis base catalysts difficult. Capitalizing on the higher nucleophilicity and basicity of our recently introduced class of isotellurourea catalysts we now succeeded in successfully employing alk-2-ynoates for asymmetric (4 + 2)-heterocycloadditions as well, resulting in even improved enantioselectivities as compared to the use of analogous allenoates. These results demonstrate the high potential of the highly reactive isotelluroureas for the catalytic activation of less reactive starting materials where established catalyst classes come to their limits.
item.dc:creator: Anna Scheucher, Mario Waser
item.dc:title: Isotellurourea‐Catalyzed Enantioselective (4+2)‐Heterocycloadditions of But‐2‐ynoates and Michael Acceptors

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Achieving Diverse Functionalizations of Electron‐Deficient Aryl Halides via Carbonate‐Mediated Electron‐Donor–Acceptor Complexes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
We report a simple and practical electron donor–acceptor complex‐mediated method that employs inexpensive carbonate bases to activate electron‐deficient aryl iodides and bromides. The protocol enables diverse transformations, including arylation, dehalogenation, deuteration, chalcogenation, and borylation, providing a versatile platform for the functionalization of aryl halide substrates.
Aryl halides are excellent precursors for functional group interconversion due to their malleability and diversity. They are also among the most common building blocks due to their use in cross‐coupling reactions. Traditional methodologies for activating these motifs make use of expensive precious metals, such as palladium, and often require the use of complex and even more expensive ligands. Electron‐donor–acceptor complexes have recently gained attention as a new photo‐induced approach for the activation of various molecular motifs, including aryl halides. Herein, further extension of these methodologies led to the development of a straightforward and simple protocol that uses inexpensive carbonate bases for the activation of electron‐deficient aryl iodides and bromides. The procedure was employed for the arylation, dehalogenation, deuteration, chalcogenation, and borylation of aryl halide starting materials.
item.content:encoded:
We report a simple and practical electron donor–acceptor complex-mediated method that employs inexpensive carbonate bases to activate electron-deficient aryl iodides and bromides. The protocol enables diverse transformations, including arylation, dehalogenation, deuteration, chalcogenation, and borylation, providing a versatile platform for the functionalization of aryl halide substrates.


Aryl halides are excellent precursors for functional group interconversion due to their malleability and diversity. They are also among the most common building blocks due to their use in cross-coupling reactions. Traditional methodologies for activating these motifs make use of expensive precious metals, such as palladium, and often require the use of complex and even more expensive ligands. Electron-donor–acceptor complexes have recently gained attention as a new photo-induced approach for the activation of various molecular motifs, including aryl halides. Herein, further extension of these methodologies led to the development of a straightforward and simple protocol that uses inexpensive carbonate bases for the activation of electron-deficient aryl iodides and bromides. The procedure was employed for the arylation, dehalogenation, deuteration, chalcogenation, and borylation of aryl halide starting materials.
item.dc:creator: Krishnakumar Sachidanandan, Cole Stenftenagel, Athul Joshy, Sébastien Laulhé
item.dc:title: Achieving Diverse Functionalizations of Electron‐Deficient Aryl Halides via Carbonate‐Mediated Electron‐Donor–Acceptor Complexes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Fast Reduction of Nitroalkenes Into Nitroalkanes Under Continuous‐Flow Chemical Conditions
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Flow chemical synthesis was employed for the reduction of nitroalkenes to the corresponding nitroalkanes using sodium borohydride as the reducing agent. The reaction proceeds in only 150 s, affording products from good‐to‐excellent yields (61–96%).
Herein, we describe an efficient and rapid continuous‐flow reduction of nitroalkenes to nitroalkanes. The reaction, promoted by sodium borohydride at room temperature, delivered the corresponding nitroalkanes in good‐to‐excellent yields, tolerating a broad range of functionalities, with a remarkably short residence time of only 150 s.
item.content:encoded:
Flow chemical synthesis was employed for the reduction of nitroalkenes to the corresponding nitroalkanes using sodium borohydride as the reducing agent. The reaction proceeds in only 150 s, affording products from good-to-excellent yields (61–96%).


Herein, we describe an efficient and rapid continuous-flow reduction of nitroalkenes to nitroalkanes. The reaction, promoted by sodium borohydride at room temperature, delivered the corresponding nitroalkanes in good-to-excellent yields, tolerating a broad range of functionalities, with a remarkably short residence time of only 150 s.
item.dc:creator: Iqra Munir, Gianmarco Simonelli, Marino Petrini, Alessandro Palmieri
item.dc:title: Fast Reduction of Nitroalkenes Into Nitroalkanes Under Continuous‐Flow Chemical Conditions

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Palladium‐Catalyzed Denitrogenative Sonogashira Coupling of Benzothiatriazine Dioxides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A palladium‐catalyzed denitrogenative Sonogashira coupling of N‐alkyl benzothiatriazine dioxides has been developed for highly efficient access to o‐alkynylbenzenesulfonamides under mild conditions. The protocol features a wide substrate scope, good functional group compatibility, and tunable reactivity by copper cocatalyst and tetrabutyl ammonium bromide (TBAB) additive.
A denitrogenative Sonogashira coupling of N‐alkyl benzothiatriazine dioxides catalyzed by 1 mol% PdCl2(PPh3)2 or 1 mol% PdCl2(PPh3)2/CuI for aryl or alkyl alkynes, respectively, has been developed for highly efficient synthesis of o‐alkynylbenzenesulfonamides. The scope of this protocol has been demonstrated by >30 examples, with yields of up to 98%, showing a good compatibility with common functional groups. In addition, a one‐pot denitrogenative alkynylation/5‐exo‐dig cyclization of N‐alkyl benzothiatriazine dioxides with aryl alkynes is also described preliminarily under the palladium/copper bimetallic catalysis in the presence of tetrabutyl ammonium bromide (TBAB) additive.
item.content:encoded:
A palladium-catalyzed denitrogenative Sonogashira coupling of N-alkyl benzothiatriazine dioxides has been developed for highly efficient access to o-alkynylbenzenesulfonamides under mild conditions. The protocol features a wide substrate scope, good functional group compatibility, and tunable reactivity by copper cocatalyst and tetrabutyl ammonium bromide (TBAB) additive.


A denitrogenative Sonogashira coupling of N-alkyl benzothiatriazine dioxides catalyzed by 1 mol% PdCl2(PPh3)2 or 1 mol% PdCl2(PPh3)2/CuI for aryl or alkyl alkynes, respectively, has been developed for highly efficient synthesis of o-alkynylbenzenesulfonamides. The scope of this protocol has been demonstrated by >30 examples, with yields of up to 98%, showing a good compatibility with common functional groups. In addition, a one-pot denitrogenative alkynylation/5-exo-dig cyclization of N-alkyl benzothiatriazine dioxides with aryl alkynes is also described preliminarily under the palladium/copper bimetallic catalysis in the presence of tetrabutyl ammonium bromide (TBAB) additive.

item.dc:title: Palladium‐Catalyzed Denitrogenative Sonogashira Coupling of Benzothiatriazine Dioxides

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Electrophilic Iodination of Heterocyclic Compounds. Recent Advances 2008–2025: Part II
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Electrophilic iodination using molecular iodine and iodide sources represents an efficient approach for accessing valuable iodinated organic compounds. This review highlights recent advances in the iodination of heterocyclic compounds from 2008 to 2025, emphasizing the role of heteroaryl iodides as important synthetic intermediates for biologically active molecules, cross‐coupling chemistry, radical reactions, pharmaceutical development, and medical imaging.
The iodination of heterocyclic compounds has significant interest in synthetic organic chemistry, as it provides versatile and efficient pathways for the synthesis of a wide range of biologically active molecules. This review aims to highlight recent advances in the iodination of heterocyclic compounds using elemental iodine or iodide‐based reagents, covering developments reported over the past 17 years.
item.content:encoded:
Electrophilic iodination using molecular iodine and iodide sources represents an efficient approach for accessing valuable iodinated organic compounds. This review highlights recent advances in the iodination of heterocyclic compounds from 2008 to 2025, emphasizing the role of heteroaryl iodides as important synthetic intermediates for biologically active molecules, cross-coupling chemistry, radical reactions, pharmaceutical development, and medical imaging.


The iodination of heterocyclic compounds has significant interest in synthetic organic chemistry, as it provides versatile and efficient pathways for the synthesis of a wide range of biologically active molecules. This review aims to highlight recent advances in the iodination of heterocyclic compounds using elemental iodine or iodide-based reagents, covering developments reported over the past 17 years.

item.dc:title: Electrophilic Iodination of Heterocyclic Compounds. Recent Advances 2008–2025: Part II

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Gold(I)‐Mediated Reactivity of Allenes With Mesoionic Nucleophiles: A Computational Exploration
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
The regioselective gold(I)‐mediated reactivity of allenes with mesoionic nucleophiles has been evaluated through computational analysis of the mechanisms involved. Instead of typical (3 + 2) pathways, sydnones and münchnones favour the formal (3 + 3) cyclizations affording substituted dihydropyridines.
We explore the factors leading to regioselective nucleophilic additions in gold(I)‐mediated reactions with allenes through DFT analysis of model systems. The feasibility of intermolecular nucleophilic attack by sydnones and münchnones was evaluated and validated by comparison to several known nucleophiles, the transition states with münchnones being kinetically accessible. The resulting intermediates selectively undergo formal (3 + 3) cyclisations instead of the more usual (3 + 2) pathways, with substituted dihydropyridines as the ultimate products. We propose that the use of mesoionic reagents in gold(I)‐mediated processes is feasible, should regioselectively access different heterocycles, and could enable the development of new methodology in gold(I)‐mediated synthesis.
item.content:encoded:
The regioselective gold(I)-mediated reactivity of allenes with mesoionic nucleophiles has been evaluated through computational analysis of the mechanisms involved. Instead of typical (3 + 2) pathways, sydnones and münchnones favour the formal (3 + 3) cyclizations affording substituted dihydropyridines.


We explore the factors leading to regioselective nucleophilic additions in gold(I)-mediated reactions with allenes through DFT analysis of model systems. The feasibility of intermolecular nucleophilic attack by sydnones and münchnones was evaluated and validated by comparison to several known nucleophiles, the transition states with münchnones being kinetically accessible. The resulting intermediates selectively undergo formal (3 + 3) cyclisations instead of the more usual (3 + 2) pathways, with substituted dihydropyridines as the ultimate products. We propose that the use of mesoionic reagents in gold(I)-mediated processes is feasible, should regioselectively access different heterocycles, and could enable the development of new methodology in gold(I)-mediated synthesis.

item.dc:title: Gold(I)‐Mediated Reactivity of Allenes With Mesoionic Nucleophiles: A Computational Exploration

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Enantioselective Access to Tetrahydroxanthenones Catalyzed by an Ionic Proline‐Histidine Dipeptide Derivative
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Dipeptide 1a catalyzed an enantioselective synthesis of tetrahydroxanthenones by the reaction of salicylaldehydes with cyclohex‐2‐en‐1‐one, in the presence of water and a Bronsted acid additive. Interestingly, the (L,L)‐dipeptide—derived using the natural forms of both amino acids—proved superior to its diastereomeric counterpart, suggesting an intrinsic role of the histidine chiral center also in the transformation, which presumably proceeds via an oxa‐Michael addition–aldol condensation sequence.
An enantioselective synthesis of tetrahydroxanthenones was achieved by the reaction of salicylaldehydes with cyclohex‐2‐en‐1‐one, catalyzed by a proline‐histidine dipeptide derived ion‐tagged organocatalyst in the presence of water and a Bronsted acid additive. The transformation, which involves an oxa‐Michael addition–aldol condensation sequence, occurs at room temperature and was successful over a range of salicylaldehydes, affording the heterotricyclic products in 33–99% yield and 29–95% ee.
item.content:encoded:
Dipeptide 1a catalyzed an enantioselective synthesis of tetrahydroxanthenones by the reaction of salicylaldehydes with cyclohex-2-en-1-one, in the presence of water and a Bronsted acid additive. Interestingly, the (L,L)-dipeptide—derived using the natural forms of both amino acids—proved superior to its diastereomeric counterpart, suggesting an intrinsic role of the histidine chiral center also in the transformation, which presumably proceeds via an oxa-Michael addition–aldol condensation sequence.


An enantioselective synthesis of tetrahydroxanthenones was achieved by the reaction of salicylaldehydes with cyclohex-2-en-1-one, catalyzed by a proline-histidine dipeptide derived ion-tagged organocatalyst in the presence of water and a Bronsted acid additive. The transformation, which involves an oxa-Michael addition–aldol condensation sequence, occurs at room temperature and was successful over a range of salicylaldehydes, affording the heterotricyclic products in 33–99% yield and 29–95% ee.

item.dc:title: Enantioselective Access to Tetrahydroxanthenones Catalyzed by an Ionic Proline‐Histidine Dipeptide Derivative

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Gallium‐Catalyzed Tandem Carbonyl–Olefin Metathesis/Dehydrogenative Coupling Enables Rapid Access to Cyclopentenes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A cationic gallium(III) catalyst enables rapid construction of substituted cyclopentenes via tandem carbonyl‐ene metathesis/Friedel–Crafts coupling. Electronic tuning of alcohol electrophiles allows access to diverse, densely functionalized rings in a single operation.
An efficient and versatile tandem carbonyl‐ene metathesis/Friedel–Crafts‐type coupling, catalyzed by the cationic gallium(III) complex [IPr·GaCl2][SbF6], provides rapid and modular access to cyclopentenes. This one‐pot protocol converts alkene‐ketones and activated alcohols into carbocycles bearing a tetrasubstituted C ═ C bond, in yields ranging from 21 to 68%, depending on the electronic nature of the electrophile generated from primary or secondary alcohols. Variation of substitution on the carbocyclic core enables access to a diverse array of functionalized ring systems from readily available precursors through a concise sequence.
item.content:encoded:
A cationic gallium(III) catalyst enables rapid construction of substituted cyclopentenes via tandem carbonyl-ene metathesis/Friedel–Crafts coupling. Electronic tuning of alcohol electrophiles allows access to diverse, densely functionalized rings in a single operation.


An efficient and versatile tandem carbonyl-ene metathesis/Friedel–Crafts-type coupling, catalyzed by the cationic gallium(III) complex [IPr·GaCl2][SbF6], provides rapid and modular access to cyclopentenes. This one-pot protocol converts alkene-ketones and activated alcohols into carbocycles bearing a tetrasubstituted C ═ C bond, in yields ranging from 21 to 68%, depending on the electronic nature of the electrophile generated from primary or secondary alcohols. Variation of substitution on the carbocyclic core enables access to a diverse array of functionalized ring systems from readily available precursors through a concise sequence.
item.dc:creator: Arnaud Espied, Djamila Azrou, Alexandre Djurovic, Régis Guillot, Aurélien Alix, Vincent Gandon, Christophe Bour
item.dc:title: Gallium‐Catalyzed Tandem Carbonyl–Olefin Metathesis/Dehydrogenative Coupling Enables Rapid Access to Cyclopentenes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Shedding Light on Iron Catalysis
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Iron‐based photoredox catalysis enables precise, green synthesis under mild conditions with high selectivity and broad functional group tolerance. Due to iron's abundance, low cost, and tunable redox properties, it is promising for pharmaceuticals and materials. This review systematically covers 2019–2025 photoinduced iron systems, offering mechanistic insights and practical guidance for sustainable catalysis.
Iron‐based photoredox catalysis has become a powerful platform for achieving precise synthetic control while aligning with the goals of green chemistry. These reactions typically proceed under mild conditions and offer broad functional group compatibility along with high selectivity. Owing to iron's natural abundance, low cost, and tunable redox properties, iron photocatalysis is particularly attractive for applications in pharmaceutical synthesis and materials science. This review provides a systematic overview of photoinduced iron‐catalyzed systems reported from 2019 to 2025. By analyzing and comparing the mechanisms and key characteristics of different reaction pathways, it aims to offer both conceptual insights and practical guidance for the development of efficient, sustainable iron‐catalyzed transformations.
item.content:encoded:
Iron-based photoredox catalysis enables precise, green synthesis under mild conditions with high selectivity and broad functional group tolerance. Due to iron's abundance, low cost, and tunable redox properties, it is promising for pharmaceuticals and materials. This review systematically covers 2019–2025 photoinduced iron systems, offering mechanistic insights and practical guidance for sustainable catalysis.


Iron-based photoredox catalysis has become a powerful platform for achieving precise synthetic control while aligning with the goals of green chemistry. These reactions typically proceed under mild conditions and offer broad functional group compatibility along with high selectivity. Owing to iron's natural abundance, low cost, and tunable redox properties, iron photocatalysis is particularly attractive for applications in pharmaceutical synthesis and materials science. This review provides a systematic overview of photoinduced iron-catalyzed systems reported from 2019 to 2025. By analyzing and comparing the mechanisms and key characteristics of different reaction pathways, it aims to offer both conceptual insights and practical guidance for the development of efficient, sustainable iron-catalyzed transformations.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Photocatalytic Phosphine‐Mediated Deoxygenative Coupling of Aspartic/Glutamic Acid Residues and Azobenzenes to Access Hydrazide‐Containing Unnatural Amino Acids
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Herein, we have developed a novel synergistic catalysis strategy combining visible‐light photocatalysis with triphenylphosphine, enabling efficient and highly selective deoxygenative coupling of aspartic/glutamic acid derivatives with azobenzene. This approach provides a general and versatile method for synthesizing a series of pharmacologically interesting hydrazide‐containing unnatural amino acids.
Herein, we have developed a novel synergistic catalysis strategy combining visible‐light photocatalysis with triphenylphosphine, enabling efficient and highly selective deoxygenative coupling of aspartic/glutamic acid derivatives with azobenzene. This approach provides a general and versatile method for synthesizing a series of pharmacologically interesting hydrazide‐containing unnatural amino acids. Mechanistic studies confirmed a radical‐based pathway and elucidated the unique role of DMAP in modulating the reaction pathway through stabilization of the critical intermediate. This approach provides a new type of amino acids residues with robust active site, which are significant organic building blocks and potential bioactivity.
item.content:encoded:

This approach provides a general and versatile method for synthesizing a series of pharmacologically interesting hydrazide-containing unnatural amino acids.


Herein, we have developed a novel synergistic catalysis strategy combining visible-light photocatalysis with triphenylphosphine, enabling efficient and highly selective deoxygenative coupling of aspartic/glutamic acid derivatives with azobenzene. This approach provides a general and versatile method for synthesizing a series of pharmacologically interesting hydrazide-containing unnatural amino acids. Mechanistic studies confirmed a radical-based pathway and elucidated the unique role of DMAP in modulating the reaction pathway through stabilization of the critical intermediate. This approach provides a new type of amino acids residues with robust active site, which are significant organic building blocks and potential bioactivity.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Machine Learning‐Driven Isolated Yield Prediction and Experimental Parameter Optimization for Ferulic Acid Synthesis
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This study developed a machine learning framework benchmarking ten algorithms for the high‐precision prediction and optimization of ferulic acid isolated yield. Shapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) analysis and Bi‐factor Partial Dependence Plots (Bi‐PDPs) were further implemented to identify critical governing factors and decode complex synergistic interactions, providing a theoretical foundation for rational experimental design.
Chemical synthesis is an essential method for obtaining high‐purity ferulic acid (FA). However, due to the inherent complexity of the Knoevenagel condensation system and the nonlinear coupling among process parameters, traditional approaches often fail to achieve precise isolated yield prediction and optimization. This study introduces a machine learning (ML)‐guided experimental optimization strategy. Ten representative algorithms, including Gradient Boosting Decision Trees (GBDT), Random Forest (RF), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), Support Vector Regression (SVR), Adaptive Boosting (AdaBoost), Categorical Boosting (CatBoost), k‐Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Linear Regression, Decision Trees (DT), and Ridge, were constructed and systematically evaluated. The results indicate that the RF model exhibits superior fitting accuracy and exhibits potential for generalization. Based on the RF model, Shapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) and Bi‐factor Partial Dependence Plots (Bi‐PDPs) were employed to quantitatively assess the influence of individual factors and to elucidate interactive effects between variables. This work establishes an efficient ML‐driven framework for the rational optimization of complex organic synthesis processes.
item.content:encoded:
This study developed a machine learning framework benchmarking ten algorithms for the high-precision prediction and optimization of ferulic acid isolated yield. Shapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) analysis and Bi-factor Partial Dependence Plots (Bi-PDPs) were further implemented to identify critical governing factors and decode complex synergistic interactions, providing a theoretical foundation for rational experimental design.


Chemical synthesis is an essential method for obtaining high-purity ferulic acid (FA). However, due to the inherent complexity of the Knoevenagel condensation system and the nonlinear coupling among process parameters, traditional approaches often fail to achieve precise isolated yield prediction and optimization. This study introduces a machine learning (ML)-guided experimental optimization strategy. Ten representative algorithms, including Gradient Boosting Decision Trees (GBDT), Random Forest (RF), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), Support Vector Regression (SVR), Adaptive Boosting (AdaBoost), Categorical Boosting (CatBoost), k-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Linear Regression, Decision Trees (DT), and Ridge, were constructed and systematically evaluated. The results indicate that the RF model exhibits superior fitting accuracy and exhibits potential for generalization. Based on the RF model, Shapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) and Bi-factor Partial Dependence Plots (Bi-PDPs) were employed to quantitatively assess the influence of individual factors and to elucidate interactive effects between variables. This work establishes an efficient ML-driven framework for the rational optimization of complex organic synthesis processes.
item.dc:creator: Guanghai Bai, Yuhui Zhou, Ran Chen, Jian Feng, Yuan Yue, Penghui Yue, Tao Zhang, Zhenyang Lin, Wanying Wang, Le Liu, Bo Zhu, Hongwei Lu, Qiang Zhang, Jinshan Li

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Diboron‐Mediated Chemoselective Radical Reduction of 4‐Acylpyridines to Alcohols Under Metal‐Free Aqueous Conditions
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A transition metal‐free protocol for the reduction of 4‐acylpyridines is developed using diborons in water. Acting as a green surrogate for classical single‐electron transfer (SET) reagents like SmI2, this system demonstrates high chemoselectivity. It reduces the carbonyl group via a radical pathway while preserving the pyridine core intact.
Radical reduction of carbonyl compounds is a powerful transformation typically achieved using single‐electron transfer (SET) reagents such as samarium diiodide (SmI2) or alkali metals. While effective, these methods often require strictly anhydrous conditions, inert atmospheres, or expensive reagents. Herein, we report a robust, transition metal‐free protocol for the radical‐type reduction of 4‐acylpyridines to secondary alcohols using diboron reagents in water. This system serves as an air‐stable surrogate for classical SET reductants. The diboron/water system operates under mild conditions, ensuring high chemoselectivity toward the exocyclic carbonyl group while preserving the heteroaromatic core. Preliminary mechanistic investigations support a radical pathway, highlighting the capability of diboron reagents to function as effective radical reductants in aqueous media.
item.content:encoded:
A transition metal-free protocol for the reduction of 4-acylpyridines is developed using diborons in water. Acting as a green surrogate for classical single-electron transfer (SET) reagents like SmI2, this system demonstrates high chemoselectivity. It reduces the carbonyl group via a radical pathway while preserving the pyridine core intact.


Radical reduction of carbonyl compounds is a powerful transformation typically achieved using single-electron transfer (SET) reagents such as samarium diiodide (SmI2) or alkali metals. While effective, these methods often require strictly anhydrous conditions, inert atmospheres, or expensive reagents. Herein, we report a robust, transition metal-free protocol for the radical-type reduction of 4-acylpyridines to secondary alcohols using diboron reagents in water. This system serves as an air-stable surrogate for classical SET reductants. The diboron/water system operates under mild conditions, ensuring high chemoselectivity toward the exocyclic carbonyl group while preserving the heteroaromatic core. Preliminary mechanistic investigations support a radical pathway, highlighting the capability of diboron reagents to function as effective radical reductants in aqueous media.

item.dc:title: Diboron‐Mediated Chemoselective Radical Reduction of 4‐Acylpyridines to Alcohols Under Metal‐Free Aqueous Conditions

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Mechanochemical Synthesis of Cysteine‐Containing Peptides via Native Chemical Ligation
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Cysteine‐containing peptides were efficiently synthesized through mechanochemical native chemical ligation.
Cysteine‐containing peptides are important building blocks for the preparation of artificially designed peptides. Therefore, the development of efficient methods for the synthesis of cysteine‐containing peptides is recognized as an important area of research. To achieve an efficient preparation method for cysteine‐containing peptides, we became interested in developing a mechanochemical native chemical ligation approach. First, amino acid thioesters, which are key reagents for native chemical ligation, were prepared in a ball‐mill reactor using a user‐friendly, odorless thiol reagent. Next, mechanochemical native chemical ligation between amino acid thioesters and cysteine derivatives was performed. As a result of these mechanochemical processes, cysteine‐containing peptides, including C‐terminal unprotected peptides, were efficiently prepared.
item.content:encoded:
Cysteine-containing peptides were efficiently synthesized through mechanochemical native chemical ligation.


Cysteine-containing peptides are important building blocks for the preparation of artificially designed peptides. Therefore, the development of efficient methods for the synthesis of cysteine-containing peptides is recognized as an important area of research. To achieve an efficient preparation method for cysteine-containing peptides, we became interested in developing a mechanochemical native chemical ligation approach. First, amino acid thioesters, which are key reagents for native chemical ligation, were prepared in a ball-mill reactor using a user-friendly, odorless thiol reagent. Next, mechanochemical native chemical ligation between amino acid thioesters and cysteine derivatives was performed. As a result of these mechanochemical processes, cysteine-containing peptides, including C-terminal unprotected peptides, were efficiently prepared.

item.dc:title: Mechanochemical Synthesis of Cysteine‐Containing Peptides via Native Chemical Ligation

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Iodine (III)‐Promoted Radical Alkenylation of Cyclic Ethers
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A green and sustainable oxidative alkenylation of simple ethers to construct allylic ethers was achieved using iodine(III) as a feasible dehydrogenation agent under KBr. Notably, open‐chain ethers were also found to be efficient coupling partners in this reaction, and this reaction is likely to proceed via a radical process.
A sustainable oxidative alkenylation of simple ethers to construct allylic ethers was achieved using iodine (III) as a feasible dehydrogenation agent in the presence of KBr. Notably, open‐chain ethers were also found to be efficient coupling partners in this reaction and are likely to proceed via a radical process. Hydrogen atom transfer from ethers to radicals is supported by both experimental and computational studies. This reaction provides good yields and broad functional group compatibility, along with simple operation and mild conditions.

Notably, open-chain ethers were also found to be efficient coupling partners in this reaction, and this reaction is likely to proceed via a radical process.


A sustainable oxidative alkenylation of simple ethers to construct allylic ethers was achieved using iodine (III) as a feasible dehydrogenation agent in the presence of KBr. Notably, open-chain ethers were also found to be efficient coupling partners in this reaction and are likely to proceed via a radical process. Hydrogen atom transfer from ethers to radicals is supported by both experimental and computational studies. This reaction provides good yields and broad functional group compatibility, along with simple operation and mild conditions.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Dibenzosuberene‐Winged Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
The twisted acene 8, which features two dibenzosuberene “wings”, was prepared by the addition of a crowded aryne to a similarly “winged” cyclopentadienone. Compound 8 is chiral, and it has a high barrier to thermal racemization (ΔG‡rac = 36 kcal/mol), but it undergoes fast photochemical racemization even in ambient light.
Two very crowded, twisted acenes with dibenzosuberene “wings”, 19,20,21,22‐tetraphenyl‐5H,14H‐bisdibenzo[3,4:6,7]cyclohepta[1,2‐a:1′, 2′‐c]naphthalene (7) and 19,28‐Diphenyl‐5H,14H‐bisdibenzo[3,4:6,7]cyclohepta[1,2‐a:1′, 2′‐c]dibenzo[h,j]anthracene (8), were prepared by addition of crowded arynes to the known bis(dibenzosubereno)cyclopentadienone 6. Their X‐ray structures showed the naphthalene and anthracene cores to have end‐to‐end twists of 32° and 67°, respectively. Compound 8 crystallized as a chiral conglomerate; thus, individual crystals contained only one enantiomer. The rate of racemization of 8 in solutions made from individual crystals was monitored by circular dichroism spectroscopy. Thermal racemization in the dark has a high barrier (t1/2 = 6.5 h at 450 K; ΔG‡rac = 36 kcal/mol), but exposure to even ambient light yields rapid racemization at room temperature. Computational studies of possible mechanisms of racemization of 7 and 8 are also reported.
item.content:encoded:
The twisted acene 8, which features two dibenzosuberene “wings”, was prepared by the addition of a crowded aryne to a similarly “winged” cyclopentadienone. Compound 8 is chiral, and it has a high barrier to thermal racemization (ΔG ‡ rac = 36 kcal/mol), but it undergoes fast photochemical racemization even in ambient light.


Two very crowded, twisted acenes with dibenzosuberene “wings”, 19,20,21,22-tetraphenyl-5H,14H-bisdibenzo[3,4:6,7]cyclohepta[1,2-a:1′, 2′-c]naphthalene (7) and 19,28-Diphenyl-5H,14H-bisdibenzo[3,4:6,7]cyclohepta[1,2-a:1′, 2′-c]dibenzo[h,j]anthracene (8), were prepared by addition of crowded arynes to the known bis(dibenzosubereno)cyclopentadienone 6. Their X-ray structures showed the naphthalene and anthracene cores to have end-to-end twists of 32° and 67°, respectively. Compound 8 crystallized as a chiral conglomerate; thus, individual crystals contained only one enantiomer. The rate of racemization of 8 in solutions made from individual crystals was monitored by circular dichroism spectroscopy. Thermal racemization in the dark has a high barrier (t 1/2 = 6.5 h at 450 K; ΔG ‡ rac = 36 kcal/mol), but exposure to even ambient light yields rapid racemization at room temperature. Computational studies of possible mechanisms of racemization of 7 and 8 are also reported.
item.dc:creator: Mingshu Xie, Xiaodong Zhang, Michael W. Johnson, Robert A. Pascal Jr
item.dc:title: Dibenzosuberene‐Winged Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Palladium‐Catalyzed Transfer Hydrocarbonylation of Alkenes and Alkynes with Formic Acid Derivatives
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This review focuses on developments in palladium‐catalyzed transfer hydrocarbonylation of unsaturated hydrocarbons. The content is organized according to the nucleophiles involved, covering four key reaction types: transfer hydroesterification, hydroaminocarbonylation, hydrothiocarbonylation, and hydrocarboxylation.
Transition‐metal‐catalyzed hydrocarbonylation constitutes a fundamental method for constructing carbonyl‐containing compounds with 100% atom economy. Despite its industrial relevance, the traditional hydrocarbonylation reaction employing pressurized CO has limited applicability in laboratory‐scale synthesis. To address this limitation, transfer hydrocarbonylation has emerged as a distinct alternative. This approach has circumvented the need for both external gaseous CO and separate nucleophiles by employing integrated transfer reagents that supply all requisite components. As a result, it has achieved high atom economy while offering enhanced operational simplicity under mild conditions. This review provides a systematic overview of palladium‐catalyzed transfer hydrocarbonylation of alkenes and alkynes. The discussion encompasses transfer hydroesterification, hydroaminocarbonylation, hydrothiocarbonylation, and hydrocarboxylation, with a particular focus on substrate scope, regioselectivities, and mechanistic insights. By integrating these developments, we provide a forward‐looking perspective on the role of this method in advancing valuable and practical synthesis of carbonyl compounds.
item.content:encoded:
This review focuses on developments in palladium-catalyzed transfer hydrocarbonylation of unsaturated hydrocarbons. The content is organized according to the nucleophiles involved, covering four key reaction types: transfer hydroesterification, hydroaminocarbonylation, hydrothiocarbonylation, and hydrocarboxylation.


Transition-metal-catalyzed hydrocarbonylation constitutes a fundamental method for constructing carbonyl-containing compounds with 100% atom economy. Despite its industrial relevance, the traditional hydrocarbonylation reaction employing pressurized CO has limited applicability in laboratory-scale synthesis. To address this limitation, transfer hydrocarbonylation has emerged as a distinct alternative. This approach has circumvented the need for both external gaseous CO and separate nucleophiles by employing integrated transfer reagents that supply all requisite components. As a result, it has achieved high atom economy while offering enhanced operational simplicity under mild conditions. This review provides a systematic overview of palladium-catalyzed transfer hydrocarbonylation of alkenes and alkynes. The discussion encompasses transfer hydroesterification, hydroaminocarbonylation, hydrothiocarbonylation, and hydrocarboxylation, with a particular focus on substrate scope, regioselectivities, and mechanistic insights. By integrating these developments, we provide a forward-looking perspective on the role of this method in advancing valuable and practical synthesis of carbonyl compounds.
item.dc:creator: Bao Gao, Hanmin Huang
item.dc:title: Palladium‐Catalyzed Transfer Hydrocarbonylation of Alkenes and Alkynes with Formic Acid Derivatives

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Electrochemical Halocyclization‐Enabled Synthesis of Tetrahydrofuran Derivatives With KBr/ KI
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A mild electrochemical strategy has been developed for synthesizing multisubstituted tetrahydrofuran derivatives via 1,5‐halocyclization of unactivated alkenols. This method employs readily available KBr/ KI as both the halogen source and supporting electrolyte, requiring no external oxidants or metal catalysts and producing H2 as the sole byproduct. This oxidant‐ and catalyst‐free protocol provides an efficient, atom‐economical route to synthetically important tetrahydrofuran (THF) scaffolds.
Tetrahydrofuran (THF) frameworks are privileged scaffolds prevalent in bioactive natural products and pharmaceuticals, yet the stereocontrolled synthesis of polysubstituted derivatives remains a significant challenge. While conventional methods often rely on transition‐metal catalysis, they are frequently hampered by issues such as β‐hydride elimination and the requirement for stoichiometric oxidants. In contrast, organic electrosynthesis offers a sustainable alternative by using electron transfer to activate substrates, eliminating the need for external oxidants and improving atom economy. Building on recent advances in electrochemical halogenation, this method proposes a novel and efficient strategy for constructing THF rings. We envisioned an electrochemical 1,5‐halocyclization of alkenols using inexpensive metal halides as dual‐function halogen sources and electrolytes. This anode‐driven process enables rapid assembly of the THF core, producing molecular hydrogen as the sole byproduct. This method represents a green, atom‐economical, and operationally simple approach to valuable O‐heterocycles.
item.content:encoded:
A mild electrochemical strategy has been developed for synthesizing multisubstituted tetrahydrofuran derivatives via 1,5-halocyclization of unactivated alkenols. This method employs readily available KBr/ KI as both the halogen source and supporting electrolyte, requiring no external oxidants or metal catalysts and producing H2 as the sole byproduct. This oxidant- and catalyst-free protocol provides an efficient, atom-economical route to synthetically important tetrahydrofuran (THF) scaffolds.


Tetrahydrofuran (THF) frameworks are privileged scaffolds prevalent in bioactive natural products and pharmaceuticals, yet the stereocontrolled synthesis of polysubstituted derivatives remains a significant challenge. While conventional methods often rely on transition-metal catalysis, they are frequently hampered by issues such as β-hydride elimination and the requirement for stoichiometric oxidants. In contrast, organic electrosynthesis offers a sustainable alternative by using electron transfer to activate substrates, eliminating the need for external oxidants and improving atom economy. Building on recent advances in electrochemical halogenation, this method proposes a novel and efficient strategy for constructing THF rings. We envisioned an electrochemical 1,5-halocyclization of alkenols using inexpensive metal halides as dual-function halogen sources and electrolytes. This anode-driven process enables rapid assembly of the THF core, producing molecular hydrogen as the sole byproduct. This method represents a green, atom-economical, and operationally simple approach to valuable O-heterocycles.
item.dc:creator: Zhenlei Zou, Jie Dong, Hui Wang, Tianle Xia, Zhaopan Yang, Weigang Zhang, Yi Wang
item.dc:title: Electrochemical Halocyclization‐Enabled Synthesis of Tetrahydrofuran Derivatives With KBr/ KI

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Advances in Transition‐Metal‐ and External Photocatalyst‐Free Photochemical Reactions
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This review summarizes recent advances in photochemical reactions that proceed without transition metals or external photocatalysts. By employing either direct excitation of substrates or photoexcitation of in situ formed electron donor–acceptor complexes, these transformations proceed via energy‐transfer, single‐electron transfer, or hydrogen atom transfer pathways, enabling efficient construction of C–C and C–heteroatom bonds under mild conditions.
In recent years, transition‐metal‐ and external photocatalyst‐free photochemical reactions have garnered significant attention as environmentally friendly and sustainable approaches to organic synthesis. These reactions rely on either direct photoexcitation of substrates or photoexcitation of electron donor–acceptor complexes to enable diverse transformations. This review summarizes representative advances reported in the recent years, organized by the construction of C–C and C–heteroatom bonds, and highlights key mechanistic features. This review aims to provide guidance and inspiration for reaction design and mechanistic exploration in this field.
item.content:encoded:
This review summarizes recent advances in photochemical reactions that proceed without transition metals or external photocatalysts. By employing either direct excitation of substrates or photoexcitation of in situ formed electron donor–acceptor complexes, these transformations proceed via energy-transfer, single-electron transfer, or hydrogen atom transfer pathways, enabling efficient construction of C–C and C–heteroatom bonds under mild conditions.


In recent years, transition-metal- and external photocatalyst-free photochemical reactions have garnered significant attention as environmentally friendly and sustainable approaches to organic synthesis. These reactions rely on either direct photoexcitation of substrates or photoexcitation of electron donor–acceptor complexes to enable diverse transformations. This review summarizes representative advances reported in the recent years, organized by the construction of C–C and C–heteroatom bonds, and highlights key mechanistic features. This review aims to provide guidance and inspiration for reaction design and mechanistic exploration in this field.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis of Novel C‐Acyl‐Glycosyl Sulfonamides via Pd‐Catalyzed Carbonylative Coupling
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A Pd‐catalyzed carbonylative coupling of 1‐iodo‐glycals with diverse sulfonamides using Mo(CO)6 enables efficient access to C‐acyl‐glycosyl sulfonamides.This robust and versatile strategy delivers novel glycoside derivatives in good to excellent yields, highlighting their potential for drug discovery.
We developed an efficient methodology for synthesizing C‐acyl‐glycosyl sulfonamides via a carbonylative coupling reaction, using 1‐iodo‐glycals as substrates. A wide range of aromatic, heteroaromatic, and aliphatic sulfonamides was explored, employing Mo(CO)6 as a safe, solid source of carbon monoxide gas. This approach enabled the preparation of novel glycosyl sulfonamides featuring a C‐acyl‐linked framework under mild reaction conditions, achieving good to excellent yields.
item.content:encoded:
A Pd-catalyzed carbonylative coupling of 1-iodo-glycals with diverse sulfonamides using Mo(CO)6 enables efficient access to C-acyl-glycosyl sulfonamides.This robust and versatile strategy delivers novel glycoside derivatives in good to excellent yields, highlighting their potential for drug discovery.


We developed an efficient methodology for synthesizing C-acyl-glycosyl sulfonamides via a carbonylative coupling reaction, using 1-iodo-glycals as substrates. A wide range of aromatic, heteroaromatic, and aliphatic sulfonamides was explored, employing Mo(CO)6 as a safe, solid source of carbon monoxide gas. This approach enabled the preparation of novel glycosyl sulfonamides featuring a C-acyl-linked framework under mild reaction conditions, achieving good to excellent yields.
item.dc:creator: João A. F. Silva, Mônica F. Z. J. Toledo, Flávia Manarin, João H. de Araujo‐Neto, Milene M. Hornink, Hélio A. Stefani
item.dc:title: Synthesis of Novel C‐Acyl‐Glycosyl Sulfonamides via Pd‐Catalyzed Carbonylative Coupling

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Advances in the Synthesis of Chromeno[4,3‐b]pyrrolidines
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Chromeno[4,3‐b]pyrrolidine derivatives are a class of compounds featuring a unique tricyclic fused scaffold and exhibiting significant biological activities. This review systematically summarizes the cyclization reactions catalyzed by various catalysts, all of which are employed for the construction of the Chromeno[4,3‐b]pyrrolidine scaffold.
Chromeno[4,3‐b]pyrrolidine derivatives are a class of compounds featuring a unique tricyclic fused scaffold and exhibiting significant biological activities. In recent years, remarkable progress has been made in developing efficient synthetic strategies for these complex structures. This review aims to systematically summarize various methods for synthesizing chromeno[4,3‐b]pyrrolidine derivatives. Based on the type of catalyst employed, we have summarized and categorized four main catalytic strategies: Brønsted base catalysis, N‐heterocyclic carbene catalysis, phosphine catalysis, and metal catalysis. The cyclization strategies and their proposed reaction mechanisms are discussed in detail, and we believe this review will provide valuable inspiration for future innovative research in this field.
item.content:encoded:
Chromeno[4,3-b]pyrrolidine derivatives are a class of compounds featuring a unique tricyclic fused scaffold and exhibiting significant biological activities. This review systematically summarizes the cyclization reactions catalyzed by various catalysts, all of which are employed for the construction of the Chromeno[4,3-b]pyrrolidine scaffold.


Chromeno[4,3-b]pyrrolidine derivatives are a class of compounds featuring a unique tricyclic fused scaffold and exhibiting significant biological activities. In recent years, remarkable progress has been made in developing efficient synthetic strategies for these complex structures. This review aims to systematically summarize various methods for synthesizing chromeno[4,3-b]pyrrolidine derivatives. Based on the type of catalyst employed, we have summarized and categorized four main catalytic strategies: Brønsted base catalysis, N-heterocyclic carbene catalysis, phosphine catalysis, and metal catalysis. The cyclization strategies and their proposed reaction mechanisms are discussed in detail, and we believe this review will provide valuable inspiration for future innovative research in this field.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Photoinduced Halogen Atom Transfer of Alkyl Halides With N‐Arylsulfenyl Phthalimides Toward Alkyl‐Aryl Sulfides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
An operationally simple photocatalytic strategy has been developed for the direct activation of alkyl halides via halogen atom transfer, enabling their coupling with N‐arylsulfenyl phthalimides to afford alkyl‐aryl sulfides. This transition‐metal‐free protocol proceeds under mild conditions and demonstrates a broad substrate scope.
The sulfenylation of organohalides has become a powerful strategy for CS bond formation. However, methods for activating alkyl halides in this context remain scarce. Herein, we report a simple photocatalytic strategy that generates alkyl radicals from alkyl halides via halogen atom transfer. These radicals subsequently couple efficiently with N‐arylsulfenyl phthalimides to afford alkyl‐aryl sulfides. This approach is transition‐metal‐free, operates under mild conditions, and exhibits a broad substrate scope.
item.content:encoded:
An operationally simple photocatalytic strategy has been developed for the direct activation of alkyl halides via halogen atom transfer, enabling their coupling with N-arylsulfenyl phthalimides to afford alkyl-aryl sulfides. This transition-metal-free protocol proceeds under mild conditions and demonstrates a broad substrate scope.


The sulfenylation of organohalides has become a powerful strategy for CS bond formation. However, methods for activating alkyl halides in this context remain scarce. Herein, we report a simple photocatalytic strategy that generates alkyl radicals from alkyl halides via halogen atom transfer. These radicals subsequently couple efficiently with N-arylsulfenyl phthalimides to afford alkyl-aryl sulfides. This approach is transition-metal-free, operates under mild conditions, and exhibits a broad substrate scope.

item.dc:title: Photoinduced Halogen Atom Transfer of Alkyl Halides With N‐Arylsulfenyl Phthalimides Toward Alkyl‐Aryl Sulfides

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Peptide Bond Formation via Anhydride Exchange‐Mediated In Situ Generation of N‐Methylimidazolium Cation
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Herein, we report a novel peptide bond formation strategy relying on anhydride exchange, which enables the in situ generation of N‐methylimidazolium cations by using trimethylacetic anhydride and catalytic N‐methylimidazole.
Herein, we report a method for peptide bond formation via anhydride exchange‐mediated in situ generation of N‐methylimidazolium cations using trimethylacetic anhydride (Piv2O) and catalytic N‐methylimidazole (NMI). It tolerates diverse amino acid side chains (e.g., −OH, indole NH) with no detectable racemization/epimerization even when dipeptides serve as electrophiles. Convergent synthesis of protected Leu‐enkephalin underscores its utility, expanding the toolbox for peptide bond formation.
item.content:encoded:
Herein, we report a novel peptide bond formation strategy relying on anhydride exchange, which enables the in situ generation of N-methylimidazolium cations by using trimethylacetic anhydride and catalytic N-methylimidazole.


Herein, we report a method for peptide bond formation via anhydride exchange-mediated in situ generation of N-methylimidazolium cations using trimethylacetic anhydride (Piv2O) and catalytic N-methylimidazole (NMI). It tolerates diverse amino acid side chains (e.g., −OH, indole NH) with no detectable racemization/epimerization even when dipeptides serve as electrophiles. Convergent synthesis of protected Leu-enkephalin underscores its utility, expanding the toolbox for peptide bond formation.
item.dc:creator: Chenchen Qi, Lulu Liu, Xiangsheng Xu, Xiaoqing Li
item.dc:title: Peptide Bond Formation via Anhydride Exchange‐Mediated In Situ Generation of N‐Methylimidazolium Cation

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Sustainable Synthesis of 2‐(Arylamino)nicotinic Acids Using Concentrated Solar Radiation
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A sustainable synthesis of 2‐(arylamino)nicotinic acids is achieved using concentrated solar radiation under catalyst‐ and solvent‐free conditions. This green method provides excellent yields of pharmaceutically relevant compounds in minutes. The protocol is successfully demonstrated on a gram scale for drugs like flunixin and clonixin, offering a rapid, energy‐efficient, and environmentally benign alternative to conventional synthetic routes.
2‐(Arylamino)nicotinic acids are significant in synthetic chemistry, particularly due to their applications in the pharmaceutical industry. An environmentally sustainable methodology has been developed for synthesizing these compounds, employing concentrated solar radiation (CSR) as a renewable energy source under catalyst‐ and solvent‐free conditions. This approach facilitates the rapid and selective synthesis of these biologically important compounds, achieving yields of up to 94% under mild conditions. Moreover, this method's synthetic applicability is demonstrated through synthesizing pharmaceutical compounds such as flunixin and clonixin on a gram scale. This eco‐friendly approach offers a promising alternative to traditional synthesis techniques and emphasizes the potential of CSR in promoting sustainable chemical processes.
item.content:encoded:
A sustainable synthesis of 2-(arylamino)nicotinic acids is achieved using concentrated solar radiation under catalyst- and solvent-free conditions. This green method provides excellent yields of pharmaceutically relevant compounds in minutes. The protocol is successfully demonstrated on a gram scale for drugs like flunixin and clonixin, offering a rapid, energy-efficient, and environmentally benign alternative to conventional synthetic routes.


2-(Arylamino)nicotinic acids are significant in synthetic chemistry, particularly due to their applications in the pharmaceutical industry. An environmentally sustainable methodology has been developed for synthesizing these compounds, employing concentrated solar radiation (CSR) as a renewable energy source under catalyst- and solvent-free conditions. This approach facilitates the rapid and selective synthesis of these biologically important compounds, achieving yields of up to 94% under mild conditions. Moreover, this method's synthetic applicability is demonstrated through synthesizing pharmaceutical compounds such as flunixin and clonixin on a gram scale. This eco-friendly approach offers a promising alternative to traditional synthesis techniques and emphasizes the potential of CSR in promoting sustainable chemical processes.

item.dc:title: Sustainable Synthesis of 2‐(Arylamino)nicotinic Acids Using Concentrated Solar Radiation

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Mechanochemical Hydrodeoxygenations
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A silver‐catalyzed mechanochemical hydrodeoxygenation with triethylsilane as reductant allows to convert ketones to the corresponding methylene derivatives in good yields after a short reaction time. Selected examples show that also alcohols, ethers, and sulfoxides can react.
In this work, a mechanochemical hydrodeoxygenation (HDO) was developed. Using silver triflate as catalyst and triethylsilane as reducing agent, 18 ketones and other substrates were hydrodeoxygenated in yields up to 92%.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Rh(III)‐Catalyzed [4+2] Annulation of Imidazoles With α‐Diazocarbonyl Compounds: Direct Access to π‐Conjugated Imidazole‐Fused Isoquinolinone
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A Rh(III)‐catalyzed [4 + 2] annulation of 2‐aryl imidazoles/benzimidazoles with α‐diazocarbonyl compounds is developed for the synthesis of π‐conjugated imidazo/benzimidazo[2,1‐a]isoquinolinones systems. Mechanistic studies and control experiment reveal that the reaction proceeds via imidazole‐directed ortho C(sp2)–H activation, followed by carbene C–H insertion and subsequent intramolecular amidation to furnish the [4 + 2] annulation product.
A Rh(III)‐catalyzed [4 + 2] annulation of 2‐aryl imidazoles/benzimidazoles with α‐diazocarbonyl compounds has been developed for the synthesis of π‐conjugated imidazo/benzimidazo[2,1‐a]isoquinolinones systems. This method exhibits a broad substrate scope and excellent functional group tolerance, enabling the access of diverse tricyclic and tetracyclic π‐extended imidazole and benzimidazole derivatives. Mechanistic studies and control experiment revealed that the reaction proceeds via imidazole‐directed ortho C(sp2)–H activation, followed by carbene C–H insertion and subsequent intramolecular amidation to furnish the [4 + 2] annulation product. An optimized scaffold demonstrated potential relevance in materials and medicinal chemistry.
item.content:encoded:
A Rh(III)-catalyzed [4 + 2] annulation of 2-aryl imidazoles/benzimidazoles with α-diazocarbonyl compounds is developed for the synthesis of π-conjugated imidazo/benzimidazo[2,1-a]isoquinolinones systems. Mechanistic studies and control experiment reveal that the reaction proceeds via imidazole-directed ortho C(sp2)–H activation, followed by carbene C–H insertion and subsequent intramolecular amidation to furnish the [4 + 2] annulation product.


A Rh(III)-catalyzed [4 + 2] annulation of 2-aryl imidazoles/benzimidazoles with α-diazocarbonyl compounds has been developed for the synthesis of π-conjugated imidazo/benzimidazo[2,1-a]isoquinolinones systems. This method exhibits a broad substrate scope and excellent functional group tolerance, enabling the access of diverse tricyclic and tetracyclic π-extended imidazole and benzimidazole derivatives. Mechanistic studies and control experiment revealed that the reaction proceeds via imidazole-directed ortho C(sp2)–H activation, followed by carbene C–H insertion and subsequent intramolecular amidation to furnish the [4 + 2] annulation product. An optimized scaffold demonstrated potential relevance in materials and medicinal chemistry.

item.dc:title: Rh(III)‐Catalyzed [4+2] Annulation of Imidazoles With α‐Diazocarbonyl Compounds: Direct Access to π‐Conjugated Imidazole‐Fused Isoquinolinone

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Pd‐Catalyzed Cyclotrimerization and Cycloaddition of 5,6‐Didehydrobenzo[8]annulenes (BenzoCOTynes): Formation of Nonplanar Tri[8]annulenes and Diels–Alder Cycloadducts
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A Pd(0)‐catalyzed cyclotrimerization of benzoCOTynes to form tri(benzo[8]annulenes) was developed. Three distinct isomers were isolated and structurally characterized by single‐crystal X‐ray diffraction. Ligand modulation proved crucial for enhancing regioselectivity: P(4‐OMePh), and (S)‐tBu‐Phox promoted the formation of the symmetric isomers. Both benzoCOTyne and benzodiCOTyne readily participated in Diels–Alder cycloadditions, affording the corresponding adducts in good yields.
We investigated the Pd(0)‐catalyzed cyclotrimerization of benzoCOTynes, which afforded a set of regioisomeric tri(benzo[8]annulene) products. Three distinct isomers were isolated and unambiguously characterized by single‐crystal X‐ray diffraction. Ligand modulation proved crucial for enhancing regioselectivity, with P(4‐OMePh)3 and (S)tBu‐Phox promoting the formation of the symmetric isomers. The resulting polycyclic frameworks exhibited high thermal stability, remaining intact up to 140°C. Furthermore, both benzoCOTyne and benzodiCOTyne readily participated in Diels–Alder cycloadditions, delivering the corresponding adducts in good yields.
item.content:encoded:
A Pd(0)-catalyzed cyclotrimerization of benzoCOTynes to form tri(benzo[8]annulenes) was developed. Three distinct isomers were isolated and structurally characterized by single-crystal X-ray diffraction. Ligand modulation proved crucial for enhancing regioselectivity: P(4-OMePh), and (S)-tBu-Phox promoted the formation of the symmetric isomers. Both benzoCOTyne and benzodiCOTyne readily participated in Diels–Alder cycloadditions, affording the corresponding adducts in good yields.


We investigated the Pd(0)-catalyzed cyclotrimerization of benzoCOTynes, which afforded a set of regioisomeric tri(benzo[8]annulene) products. Three distinct isomers were isolated and unambiguously characterized by single-crystal X-ray diffraction. Ligand modulation proved crucial for enhancing regioselectivity, with P(4-OMePh)3 and (S)tBu-Phox promoting the formation of the symmetric isomers. The resulting polycyclic frameworks exhibited high thermal stability, remaining intact up to 140°C. Furthermore, both benzoCOTyne and benzodiCOTyne readily participated in Diels–Alder cycloadditions, delivering the corresponding adducts in good yields.

item.dc:title: Pd‐Catalyzed Cyclotrimerization and Cycloaddition of 5,6‐Didehydrobenzo[8]annulenes (BenzoCOTynes): Formation of Nonplanar Tri[8]annulenes and Diels–Alder Cycloadducts

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Advances in Synthesis and Applications of Azoxybenzenes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
The synthesis approaches to access azoxybenzenes employing thermal, photo‐redox mechanical and electrochemical conditions have been discussed. Their applications in key reactions including nitrene transfer reactions, azoxy group transfer, heterocyclic synthesis, and as directing groups in catalysis have been explored describing the recent trends and mechanistic interventions.
Azoxybenzene derivatives are important scaffolds in organic chemistry. These are widely used molecules having diverse bioactivity and several applications including fine chemicals synthesis, heterocyclic scaffolds synthesis, as directing groups in functionalization reactions and use as organic dyes. These are the vital intermediates observed during the redox pathways for the nitrobenzene to aniline conversions and vice‐versa. It is quite challenging to extract these intermediates as the final product from these redox pathways. Herein, we have focused on the synthesis approaches to access azoxybenzenes employing thermal, photo‐redox mechanical, and electrochemical conditions. Additionally, their applications in key reactions including nitrene transfer reactions, azoxy group transfer, heterocyclic synthesis, and as directing groups in catalysis have been explored describing the recent trends and mechanistic interventions.
item.content:encoded:
The synthesis approaches to access azoxybenzenes employing thermal, photo-redox mechanical and electrochemical conditions have been discussed. Their applications in key reactions including nitrene transfer reactions, azoxy group transfer, heterocyclic synthesis, and as directing groups in catalysis have been explored describing the recent trends and mechanistic interventions.
